source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
section: The Illusion of the Good Parent
text:
A journey from modern parenting to biological alignment

by Geoffrey Owen

Dedication

For every parent who think they are doing it wrong.

Epigraph

Parenting was never meant to be a performance. It was always meant to be a relationship.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
There was a time when parenting was less something we did and more something we simply lived.
Children grew alongside adults who rarely used the word “parenting” at all. They carried, guided, included, and trusted that maturity would unfold as nature intended.

Today, the landscape is different. Parenting has become an identity, a role to perform, a measure of worth. Shelves overflow with advice, podcasts promise calmer homes, and courses teach resilience as though love were a curriculum. Each offers hope, and for a while, they help. Yet beneath the reassurance lies a quiet unease. Parents are working harder than ever and feeling less confident. The harder they try to get it right, the more distant ease seems to become.

You may recognise this in your own home: the small tensions over ordinary things, the pressure to manage emotions, to keep the peace, to say the right words. It can feel as if every moment is a test you might fail. Beneath the daily effort lingers an invisible question: If I love my child so deeply, why does this still feel so hard?

Modern parenting was built on the promise that enough knowledge would remove the struggle. If we just read the right books, used the right phrases, and managed behaviour correctly, harmony would follow. But what if the difficulty lies not in what we lack, but in what we have learned to overdo? What if we have mistaken performance for presence, and management for maturity?

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
This book begins with that question. Because somewhere between the advice, the science, and the endless striving, we lost something quietly essential — the naturalness of being with a child. We have become so accustomed to strategies that we rarely ask whether the human design ever required them at all.

You do not need to discard everything you know. You simply need to look again — gently, curiously — through a lens that biology and ancient wisdom have held open all along. The story of modern parenting is not one of failure. It is a story of forgetting. And what we have forgotten is how deeply we are built to belong to one another.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
section: Why Parenting Feels Harder Than It Should
text:
The modern world has redefined what it means to be a good parent. Success is now measured in calm responses, consistent routines, and productive outcomes. The cultural script tells us that our job is to shape, teach, and manage. It sounds noble, but hidden within it is an assumption that maturity is something parents must produce rather than something that naturally unfolds.

This belief grew from the soil of the twentieth century. Behavioural psychology promised predictability: reward what you want, discourage what you do not. Factories and classrooms mirrored the same logic. Efficiency became a virtue, and control became the measure of competence. In this climate, doing became proof of love.

Parents absorbed this worldview without realising. Effort equalled care. Structure equalled safety. Explanation equalled understanding. Yet children are not built on logic alone. Their systems read rhythm, tone, and presence long before they interpret words. What feels like guidance to an adult can feel like pressure to a child. The distance grows not from neglect, but from too much management.

The tragedy is that this disconnection feels normal. We are told that exhaustion, guilt, and self-doubt are inevitable, proof that we care enough. But biology tells a different story. Children thrive not through control but through alignment — through the invisible synchrony that arises when adult and child are at ease with one another. The more we chase improvement, the more that synchrony slips away.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
section: Why Parenting Feels Harder Than It Should
text: To question this story is not rebellion; it is remembering. It takes courage to pause amid the noise and ask whether parenting was ever meant to be this heavy. Beneath the layers of culture and advice lies an older truth waiting to be rediscovered: being, not doing, is the foundation of growth.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Long before experts, strategies, or reward charts, human beings parented through attunement. Infants were carried close enough to feel heartbeat and breath. Toddlers learned through observation and inclusion. Elders modelled calm through rhythm rather than instruction. Maturity arose from participation, not control.

Jean Liedloff, observing tribal communities in South America, noticed that children rarely appeared defiant or disoriented. They were seldom corrected, yet they matured with emotional steadiness. What she saw was not a method but a context: adults who trusted biology’s design. They did not separate parenting from life; they embodied steadiness within it.

Modern neuroscience affirms what Liedloff intuited. John Bowlby’s attachment theory and Stephen Porges’ polyvagal research point to the same principle: safety is relational, not instructional. A child’s nervous system calibrates to the adult’s state. Calm cannot be taught; it must be transmitted. Regulation flows downward, not upward.

Parenting therefore begins, quite literally, in the body. The parent’s nervous system becomes the environment in which the child’s development unfolds. The tone of your voice, the rhythm of your day, the presence you bring to each moment — all communicate safety or threat. When alignment exists, growth occurs naturally. When it does not, the child must adapt, often in ways that appear as defiance or withdrawal.

This book invites you to return to that original design. Nature has already provided the blueprint. Most modern advice, while well-intentioned, has simply drifted away from it. The illusion of the good parent is not that love is false, but that love has been overcomplicated. Beneath all the doing, the essence remains simple: children grow through who we are being.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
If you have felt weary, it is not because you are doing it wrong. It is because you have been trying to do what was never meant to be done alone. The village that once held parents has disappeared, replaced by comparison, commentary, and quiet isolation. No wonder exhaustion feels like the price of love. But love does not ask for depletion; it asks for presence.

Presence begins when you stop measuring. It begins the moment you trust that relationship, not routine, is what steadies a child. This does not mean abandoning boundaries or structure; it means remembering their purpose — to contain safety, not to prove control. When your actions arise from being rather than anxiety, parenting becomes less about correction and more about coherence.

You may have glimpsed this already: mornings that begin with connection instead of command, nights when listening replaces lecture, the quiet relief when you realise your child is not broken and neither are you. These moments are not exceptions; they are the design trying to reassert itself.

As you move through the chapters ahead, you will see how the illusion of the good parent began, how the doing mindset took hold, and how biology has been waiting patiently beneath it all. You will not find a new method here. You will find a way of seeing that makes methods unnecessary. The journey is not about improvement, but alignment — a return to what love has always known.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
What does “good parent” mean to you, and where did that idea come from?
When do you feel most natural with your child?
When does love start to feel like pressure?
How do you know when effort has replaced ease?
What might change if you trusted biology more than advice?
If you stopped doing for a moment, what would being sound like in your home?

Perhaps the good parent was never meant to be invented… only remembered.

In the first chapter, we will look more closely at how the “good parent” ideal took shape: how culture turned love into labour, how control replaced connection, and why recognising the illusion is the first act of freedom.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
There are few roles more heavily freighted with expectation than being a parent. From the moment a child arrives, a quiet current of evaluation begins. Are you doing enough, saying the right things, giving them every opportunity? Books, podcasts, and social media assure you that good parenting is a skill that can be mastered, a series of steps that, if followed precisely, will yield a confident and contented child. Each new idea offers hope, and for a brief time the household feels steadier. Yet the peace rarely lasts. The same struggles reappear, and you are left wondering why the most natural relationship in the world requires so much effort.

You try to stay patient. You explain calmly, offer choices, use the language of empathy. Still the tone of defiance returns, the tearful collapse, the silence after words you never meant to say. At night you replay each moment, searching for the place where things went wrong. You promise to do better tomorrow. The self-correction becomes a ritual of love and guilt intertwined.

Modern culture quietly reinforces this rhythm. You are told that parenting is hard work, that exhaustion is proof of devotion, that boundaries must be firm and emotions managed. Struggle is recast as virtue. The good parent is not the one who rests easily in relationship, but the one who never stops trying. The harder you strive, the more moral it feels, even as the sense of connection grows thinner.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
In quieter moments, you may sense the contradiction. You work hard to stay calm, yet your child turns away. You use reason, yet they respond with tears. The technique succeeds briefly, then dissolves. You are left with the uneasy thought that doing everything right still feels wrong. Perhaps, you think, there is something missing that no amount of effort can replace.

For generations, parents assumed this was simply the human condition. Children resist; parents persist. But the dissonance you feel may not be a flaw in you or in them. It may be the signal of a culture that replaced the original design of relationship with a performance of control. The illusion of the good parent was not born from love but from misunderstanding: the belief that love must always prove itself through action.

Jean Liedloff once wrote that babies arrive expecting the world to feel a certain way—warm, rhythmic, continuous. When those expectations are met, they rest. When they are not, they adapt through protest or withdrawal. The same principle holds beyond infancy. A child’s system listens less to words than to the emotional climate surrounding them. When that climate feels managed rather than lived, the body reads uncertainty. The child reacts, not to defy, but to restore equilibrium.

Parents often interpret this reaction as disobedience. Culture encourages that view. The language of behaviour—compliance, consequence, reward—frames relationship as management. You begin to believe that your task is to engineer outcomes, to shape character as though it were clay. Yet biology does not respond to management. It responds to attunement.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Werner Erhard distinguished between content and context: what you do versus the space from which you do it. Transformation, he taught, comes not from altering the content but from shifting the context. Parenting obeys the same law. You can change the techniques endlessly, but if the context remains one of control, the feeling in the home remains the same.

The modern parent, without ever intending it, often lives inside this inherited context. You think you are guiding, but you are managing. You think you are teaching, but you are performing. The culture rewards performance; it seldom values presence. And because performance is tiring, you begin to equate exhaustion with goodness. The illusion deepens.

Behind this illusion lies a forgotten truth: children are not designed to be controlled; they are designed to be connected. They grow not through the precision of adult actions but through the stability of adult being. When that being wavers, no strategy can compensate. What the child needs most is not a better system but a steadier atmosphere—the quiet assurance that the person leading them is anchored, even when the day is not.

You may already sense how radical this is. It suggests that parenting is not primarily a task but a relationship that reveals who you are. The good parent ideal focuses on doing. The real work begins with being. To see this clearly is to glimpse the edge of the illusion itself.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
The story of how that illusion took hold begins a century ago, in an age obsessed with predictability. Early psychologists such as B. F. Skinner believed that human behaviour could be shaped through systems of reinforcement. Their experiments with animals were intended to illuminate learning; instead they reshaped culture. Schools, workplaces, and homes all absorbed the same lesson: behaviour could be engineered if one applied the right consequences.

By the middle of the twentieth century this behavioural model had seeped into everyday parenting. Good behaviour was to be rewarded, bad behaviour discouraged. Emotions were treated as variables to be managed rather than messages to be understood. The family became a miniature laboratory of control. Parents measured success by obedience rather than connection.

This shift was subtle but profound. It redefined maturity itself. Instead of seeing growth as something that arises naturally from secure attachment, society came to see it as something to be imposed through discipline and instruction. Parenting became less about relationship and more about outcome.

Over time, this new logic merged with consumer culture. If behaviour could be engineered, then so could happiness. Parenting books multiplied, each promising measurable results: calmer mornings, compliant toddlers, confident teens. Love became procedural, expressed through steps and strategies. The parent’s presence—tone, rhythm, patience—was treated as secondary, almost incidental.

John Bowlby, writing against this tide, reminded us that a child’s need for closeness is not a stage to be overcome but the foundation of development. Decades later, Mary Ainsworth’s “secure base” studies confirmed that children explore most freely not when pushed away but when held in emotional proximity. These findings quietly contradicted the behavioural creed. Yet culture had already moved on, seduced by the promise of control.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
In daily life the cost of this promise becomes clear. Parents discover that rewards lose their power and consequences breed resentment. The harder they push, the more fragile the relationship feels. The illusion whispers that better technique will solve it, but biology tells another story. A child’s system reads inconsistency and reacts not out of defiance but out of uncertainty. Misbehaviour, in this light, is not rebellion but communication.

Stephen Porges later explained this through the language of the nervous system. The body, he said, constantly scans for safety. When a parent’s tone tightens or eyes harden, the child’s physiology detects threat long before words are spoken. No amount of logic can override this sensory truth. Safety is felt, not reasoned. To a child, parental calm is not an instruction but an atmosphere.

Epigenetic research now extends this understanding further. The emotional climate of a household influences which genes are expressed in a child’s developing brain. Stress hormones, nurturance, and rhythm all leave biochemical traces. Biology listens to belonging. It listens not to what we say, but to who we are being.

Once you begin to see this, the pieces align. The illusion of the good parent is not simply emotional—it is historical, cultural, and physiological. It persists because it flatters effort while ignoring alignment. The invitation, then, is not to work harder but to see differently. Parenting is not a contest of endurance; it is the art of coherence.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
And coherence begins when you remember that your presence is the method. The child’s system does not ask for perfection; it asks for steadiness. When that steadiness returns, so does ease. The illusion fades, and what remains is what was always meant to be there: relationship, unperformed and real.

What Biology Tells Us — The Design We Forgot

When you look at children through the lens of biology, everything begins to make sense. Human infants arrive already tuned to relationship. They seek rhythm, tone, and the safety of proximity. From their first moments they scan the environment for cues of calm or tension, adjusting their tiny systems to match. Nature assumed an unbroken connection between adult and child. Only recently has culture interrupted that flow.

Jean Liedloff observed this harmony among the Yekuana people in South America, where babies were carried throughout the day and included in adult life. There were few tantrums, little defiance, and no need for elaborate techniques. Liedloff concluded that children come equipped with expectations about how life should feel. When those expectations are met, development unfolds effortlessly; when they are denied, behaviour becomes the language of protest.

Modern science continues to affirm what she saw. John Bowlby’s attachment theory and Mary Ainsworth’s later studies showed that children explore the world most freely when they are certain of closeness. Their need for connection is not weakness but the foundation of independence. As we noted earlier, these insights quietly opposed the behaviourist view that obedience produces maturity.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Stephen Porges added another layer with his polyvagal theory. The nervous system, he explained, constantly listens for signs of safety. A soft tone, relaxed facial muscles, and steady breathing tell a child that the world is secure. A clipped voice or anxious body tells them the opposite. The response is automatic, not deliberate. When safety feels uncertain, the body prepares to fight, flee, freeze, or please.

This means that much of what we call misbehaviour is physiology in action. A child who refuses may not be oppositional; they may be signalling that their system has moved into defence. Our job is not to suppress the signal but to understand it. When the parent’s presence restores calm, the child’s biology returns to balance. Connection, not correction, resolves the storm.

Epigenetics gives this understanding a molecular foundation. The emotional tone of a household influences how genes involved in stress and regulation are expressed. Warmth and rhythm promote resilience; chronic tension leaves traces that echo through development. Biology listens, moment by moment, to the atmosphere we create.

Seen this way, parenting is less about shaping behaviour and more about becoming the environment in which behaviour organises itself. The steadier the environment, the less the need for control. When the adult body offers coherence, the child’s body mirrors it. This is the quiet miracle hidden beneath the noise of modern advice.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Parents often discover this truth by accident. They stop mid-argument, take a breath, and notice their child softening too. Nothing was said; everything changed. The nervous systems aligned before the words caught up. That is biology remembering its design. It does not require mastery, only presence.

The extraordinary relief in this realisation is that you do not have to teach maturity; you have to allow it. Growth is not manufactured through instruction but invited through steadiness. Children are already oriented towards wholeness. They simply need adults who can hold the space while that wholeness unfolds.

Reflection and Realisation — Seeing Beyond the Trap

Questioning what you have always believed about parenting can feel unsettling. You may have built your sense of self around being calm, patient, and consistent, only to discover that those ideals belong to a cultural story rather than a biological truth. Yet the moment you see the illusion, something gentler begins to appear in its place.

Perhaps you can remember a time when your child reacted strongly and nothing you said seemed to help. You may have felt small, ashamed, or furious. Looking back through this new lens, can you sense that their reaction was never about you at all? It was their biology seeking balance in an atmosphere that felt uncertain. What looked like rejection may have been a plea for safety.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
When this understanding lands, control begins to loosen its grip. You start to listen differently—to your child, to yourself, to the subtle signals that run beneath words. The home becomes less a stage for performance and more a place of shared humanity. You notice that calm cannot be performed; it must be real. Children sense the difference instantly.

Parents who embrace this shift often describe a feeling of spaciousness. The endless self-critique softens. Effort turns into presence. Boundaries remain, but they carry warmth instead of tension. Discipline becomes guidance rather than threat. The family atmosphere steadies, not because everyone behaves perfectly, but because everyone feels seen.

It takes courage to live this way. The world still rewards busyness and performance. You will be tempted to measure yourself by outcomes, to compare, to return to the safety of doing. But once you have glimpsed alignment, the old methods lose their shine. You begin to sense that peace is not something you enforce; it is something you embody.

This realisation is not a finish line but a beginning. Awareness will fade and return many times. Each return deepens the capacity to stay grounded. Parenting becomes less about perfection and more about practice—the daily choice to meet life as it is, not as the manuals promised it would be.

In this way, the illusion of the good parent serves a purpose. It exhausts the strategies that never worked, clearing space for something true. The moment you see that effort alone cannot create love, you are free to experience love as it was always meant to be: unearned, uncomplicated, enough.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
The journey from doing to being does not erase the challenges; it changes their meaning. Frustration becomes feedback, not failure. Defiance becomes dialogue. Every clash becomes another chance to steady yourself and offer the calm your child’s system is waiting for. The illusion loses its power the instant you see it for what it is—a story ready to be outgrown.

When that happens, parenting stops feeling like performance and starts feeling like relationship again. The search for the perfect technique fades, replaced by the quiet confidence that who you are being is already the lesson your child needs most.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Take a quiet moment to recall a time today when you were trying hard to get something right.
What might your child’s biology have been experiencing in that moment?
Where has effort begun to replace ease in your relationship?
What happens inside you when you stop managing and start listening?
If you trusted that presence communicates more than words, how would that change your tone, your pace, your day?
What would it feel like to let love be simple again?

What if love, when it tries too hard, forgets how to be love at all?

You may already sense the next question forming. If parenting is not about doing more, why is doing so hard to stop? In the next chapter we will look at how modern life turned love into labour, why effort feels safer than ease, and how the shift from doing to being begins to restore alignment in everyday moments.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
You may not notice how quickly you move into doing. The moment your child cries, you reach for a fix. A small disagreement becomes a plan, a checklist, a lesson. Activity feels like love in motion and, for a while, it works: the household steadies, the tension recedes. Yet underneath the rhythm of achievement lies fatigue. The effort that once felt noble begins to carry strain.

Doing is the reflex of modern life. It promises certainty in a world that rarely feels predictable. The more we act, the safer we imagine ourselves to be. Solving discomfort through motion becomes second nature, talk more, reason more, schedule more. But a nervous system that equates movement with safety never truly rests. In parenting, this becomes an invisible race to prove that you are enough.

You might recognise the habit. Even in calm moments you find yourself scanning ahead, anticipating friction, rehearsing explanations that might prevent it. The body stays slightly braced, eyes on what could go wrong next. Biology interprets vigilance as care, yet it costs presence. Beneath the noise of busyness lies a quieter need…to be still, to feel at ease with the child beside you.

Culture rewards visible effort. The parent who tries hardest is praised; the one who appears relaxed risks being judged. Exhaustion becomes a badge of sincerity. But when love becomes performance, intimacy thins. The child senses the strain behind the smile. Their own system mirrors that restless energy, learning that closeness means pressure.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
This reflex is not a flaw of character but an inheritance. We were raised within systems that valued productivity over presence. At school we learned that speed mattered more than understanding; in work we learned that output mattered more than wellbeing. It is natural that we would bring those lessons home. Family life begins to sound like management, its silences filled with advice.

Biologically, continual doing sustains the stress response. Cortisol sharpens focus but dulls empathy. The capacity to notice subtle cues shrinks. The more you try to perfect your responses, the less you perceive what your child actually needs. Effort blinds sensitivity; precision replaces perception.

The paradox deepens: the harder you strive for control, the less control you feel. Every success seems temporary; each calm moment feels fragile. Beneath the hum of activity lives unease—the fear that stillness might expose something you cannot fix by doing. Yet what stillness truly reveals is simplicity: connection was never lost, only buried under movement.

Parents sometimes stumble upon this truth unexpectedly. A child cries and, without thinking, you gather them close. No teaching, no reasoning, just contact. Their breathing slows; yours follows. Time expands. In that instant, biology re-aligns. Doing ceases, being begins. It is not technique that restores harmony but presence.

Stillness can feel alien because we have trained ourselves to mistrust it. Quiet feels like neglect, inaction like indifference. Yet love is often most audible in the pause. To a nervous system attuned to speed, that pause feels radical. In it, both parent and child rediscover what safety sounds like.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text: The doing mindset disguises itself as care. It insists that one more plan, one more conversation, one more effort will bring peace. But peace never arises from greater efficiency. It arrives the moment we remember that neither life nor love were problems to be solved.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
section: How Doing Became the Measure of Love (part 1)
text:
The roots of the doing mindset reach into the history of progress. When industry began timing every motion, productivity became the moral measure of worth. Frederick Taylor’s experiments with efficiency shaped not only factories but the imagination of an age. If every process could be improved, why not people? In time that belief entered homes. Worth became linked to output; rest became suspect.

Behavioural psychology gave this worldview scientific weight. B. F. Skinner’s research suggested that behaviour could be shaped through reward and consequence. The lesson spread quickly. Teachers, employers, and parents alike came to see management as love in action. If you could produce obedience, you were succeeding. The disorder of human feeling seemed finally conquered by method.

As we saw earlier in Bowlby’s work, the human story does not follow that script. He showed that children’s primary drive is for attachment, not approval. Yet the behavioural creed, simpler and more measurable, won cultural authority. It entered textbooks, advertising, and daily conversation until control itself began to feel like compassion.

Sociologist Arlie Hochschild later described how this ethic colonised family life. Home became an extension of the workplace. Parents spoke of “quality time” as though affection were an appointment. Even leisure needed to prove its value. Doing replaced being as the currency of care.

Technology has amplified this inheritance. Social media rewards visible activity, posts, milestones, achievements. Quiet attunement leaves no record and therefore seems less real. Parents begin to believe that love must be displayed to exist. The pressure to perform intimacy creates a subtle distance from the very people we wish to reach.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
section: How Doing Became the Measure of Love (part 2)
text:
This logic shapes childhood too. From their earliest lessons, children are rewarded for output. Stickers, scores, and gold stars turn curiosity into competition. By adolescence, many have absorbed the adult creed: I do, therefore I am enough. Achievement becomes the fragile substitute for belonging.

Yet the body keeps older wisdom. Long before industry and metrics, communities survived through rhythm, cooperation, and presence. Human biology still expects that pace. When it is replaced by relentless motion, the organism protests. What we label anxiety or defiance often signals a nervous system stretched beyond its natural tempo.

Doing feels safer than being because it promises control. Control offers the illusion of certainty, and certainty soothes fear. Yet a family built on control grows tight; the space for spontaneity vanishes. Every attempt to perfect love constricts it further. The more we manage, the less we meet.

Parents who recognise this pattern often find themselves slowing down by necessity rather than choice. Exhaustion forces a pause. In that pause, something unexpected happens: warmth returns. Meals take longer, laughter reappears, silence loses its edge. Doing still occurs, laundry folded, lessons learned, but it is no longer the measure of love; it becomes its natural expression.

When we remember that being is the source from which doing draws meaning, love regains its proportion. The home turns from a project into a rhythm. Effort remains, but it moves with grace instead of strain. That is the beginning of alignment.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
section: How Doing Became the Measure of Love (part 3)
text:
Being as Biology’s Language

Biology speaks a language older than words. A child’s body listens not to logic but to atmosphere, the tone of a voice, the rhythm of breath, the presence or absence of ease. When these cues are steady, the child’s system relaxes; when they fluctuate, the system braces. We may explain, persuade, or reason, yet the nervous system has already answered. It trusts what it feels long before it believes what it hears.

Stephen Porges’ polyvagal theory helps us understand this. The nervous system continually scans for signals of safety or threat. When a parent’s tone carries tension, the child’s body detects it immediately. When the parent softens, safety is restored without a word. Regulation travels down the line of relationship. Calm cannot be performed; it must be embodied.

Jean Liedloff witnessed this biological conversation among the Yequanna, where adults rarely interrupted children’s natural rhythms. Doing was woven into being; carrying, tending, and teaching happened side by side. There was no separation between task and relationship. Modern parents, by contrast, have learned to divide life into roles like worker, partner, parent, as though connection required a different skill set for each.

John Bowlby’s work on attachment, which we touched on earlier, shows that the drive for proximity is the foundation of exploration. When a child feels securely connected, curiosity flourishes. When the bond feels uncertain, exploration retreats. This is not psychology; it is physiology. Safety is the soil in which growth takes root.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
section: How Doing Became the Measure of Love (part 4)
text:
Presence therefore becomes the parent’s primary task. It is not the stillness of inactivity but the steadiness of attention. When your presence is anchored, your child’s biology reads coherence; when it wavers, theirs compensates. Every gesture, tone, and glance participates in this silent dialogue.

Daniel Siegel later called this process “attunement”, the moment when one nervous system resonates with another. It is the body’s version of empathy, a wordless calibration that builds trust. You cannot fake attunement; it happens only when the adult is self-regulated enough to notice.

The more attunement replaces management, the less dramatic parenting becomes. There are still boundaries and consequences, but they carry warmth instead of threat. The child senses that discipline comes from steadiness, not from anxiety. Behaviour changes because safety returns, not because fear prevails.

This biological language explains why doing more rarely helps. When we add explanation to agitation, the words land on a closed channel. Only when the channel opens, through tone, breath, or a gentle pause—do the words begin to matter. Being prepares the ground for every successful act of doing.

The gift of this understanding is simplicity. You do not need to invent a new technique; you need to remember an old one. Your own body is the message. When it speaks safety, love translates itself.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Letting go of constant doing can feel like abandonment. It can seem as though you are withdrawing effort, stepping back from responsibility. Yet the opposite is true. To stop doing reflexively is to reclaim authority. It is choosing presence over panic, depth over display.

The first step is honesty. Notice when you act from anxiety rather than intention. Notice how quickly a small unease becomes a flurry of correction. In those moments, pause long enough to feel your own breath. The pause is not indulgence; it is leadership. It tells the child, “We are safe. I can wait.”

Parents who experiment with this discover a strange paradox: less doing produces more peace. When space replaces speech, children settle faster. The nervous system responds to atmosphere, not argument. The silence between words becomes the medium through which safety travels.

This courage to do less runs against everything culture teaches. Productivity has become a moral value; idleness, a flaw. Yet all living systems depend on cycles of activity and rest. Without recovery, rhythm collapses. Families are no different. The house that never stops doing eventually forgets how to breathe.

Sometimes courage means letting discomfort pass without immediate repair. Tears do not always require teaching; frustration does not always need fixing. A child learns self-regulation by borrowing yours. When you remain calm in their storm, they learn that emotion can be survived. That lesson outlasts any lecture.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Repair still matters, but repair grows from authenticity, not performance. When you do speak, speak simply: “I was tense; I see it now.” The honesty resets the field. Being replaces doing once again. Each return strengthens trust on both sides.

Over time, this rhythm becomes the family’s new normal. Effort no longer feels heroic; ease no longer feels suspicious. The house breathes. Days still fill with tasks, but they unfold at human speed. Doing arises naturally from being, just as movement follows breath.

This practice is not passivity. It is active leadership grounded in awareness. You act when action serves connection; you pause when action would sever it. The difference lies in consciousness, not in busyness. That is the quiet mastery biology has always intended.

To live this way requires faith—the faith that love will hold without constant management. But every time you test it, the evidence grows. The child relaxes; you recover; the home begins to sound like harmony again. Doing becomes what it was meant to be: a servant of love, not its proof.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Where in your day does doing replace listening?
What happens in your body when you stop fixing and start noticing?
How does your child respond to silence compared with speech?
When was the last time effort felt like peace rather than strain?
What would change if you trusted that being was already enough?
How might your home sound if love no longer had to prove itself?

Sometimes the most powerful thing a parent can do is to stop doing and simply stay present.

You may already sense how doing and calmness intertwine, how easily we try to act calm in the same way we try to act in control. In the next chapter we will explore why children sense the difference, how authenticity regulates more deeply than composure, and why your body tells the truth long before your words do.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Parents often describe a kind of tiredness that sleep does not touch. It is the fatigue of holding themselves together, of staying calm no matter what happens. Modern advice praises composure as the highest form of love. You learn to lower your voice, to count to ten, to model serenity. Yet beneath the measured tone lies tension, a fine tremor that the body cannot hide. Calm becomes something to perform rather than something to live.

You may have noticed it. The moment you decide to stay calm, the shoulders tighten. You think before every word, measuring tone, editing emotion. Inside, frustration hums. You keep your voice level and hope that will be enough. But children are radar, not mirrors. They do not reflect your words; they detect your truth. They sense the current running underneath.

This is not defiance; it is attunement. A child’s nervous system is built to read authenticity. When what you feel and what you show are out of sync, the signal confuses them. They respond to the dissonance, not the discipline. What looks like resistance is often their biology searching for coherence. They cannot rest in energy that pretends.

Culture reinforces the performance. Calmness is marketed as mastery. Anger and despair are recast as failure. Entire industries exist to help parents hide what they feel. Yet genuine calm is not the absence of emotion; it is the integration of emotion. Peace is not neutrality. It is wholeness that can contain sadness, irritation, and joy without denying any of them.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Stephen Porges’ work on the polyvagal system shows why forced composure unsettles children. The vagus nerve scans micro-expressions and vocal tones for signs of threat. A controlled smile or clipped softness tells the child’s body, “Something is wrong.” Their heart rate rises, muscles stiffen, and they prepare to defend. The lesson arrives beneath language: calm can be dangerous if it is false.

Jean Liedloff saw the opposite in the Yequanna community. Adults there were not calm because they suppressed emotion but because they were congruent. Their inner state matched their outer expression. A raised voice meant annoyance, a quiet voice meant ease. Children trusted them because nothing was hidden. There was no performance to decode.

The calm you fake demands enormous energy. It teaches the body to divide itself: emotion pushed down, expression polished for display. Over time the split becomes habitual. The cost appears as tension headaches, shallow breathing, and emotional distance. You may feel hollow after moments that were meant to feel noble. Pretending calm erodes connection both with your child and with yourself.

Real calm is not control; it is containment. It begins when you acknowledge the feeling and stay present anyway. Saying, “I’m frustrated, but I’m here,” teaches more emotional intelligence than any lecture on empathy. The child learns that feelings can be felt safely and that relationship does not vanish when emotions surface. That is true regulation.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Practising authenticity requires courage. It means trusting that truth will not break the bond. The first attempts may feel clumsy. Voices tremble, tears appear, yet something real emerges. The atmosphere lightens because everyone can finally breathe. Presence replaces performance; safety returns through honesty.

Next time you feel the impulse to compose yourself into silence, pause instead. Let the breath move naturally. Let your tone match your heart. Imperfect calm is infinitely more healing than perfection that hides the truth. Children do not need parents who never lose composure; they need parents who can find themselves again.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
A child’s first language is the body. Long before speech, they are fluent in tone, rhythm, gaze and posture. Your nervous system becomes their weather. Every sigh, every softening of the shoulders, every clipped breath communicates safety or threat, and their system adjusts accordingly. Calm is not something you do to a child, it is something they borrow from you.

John Bowlby showed that children use the caregiver as a secure base. When that base feels steady, exploration blossoms. When it feels uncertain, behaviour narrows into clinging, avoidance, or opposition. As we noted earlier, steadiness cannot be faked. The body’s truth is what counts, and a child always reads the truth first.

Mary Ainsworth’s observations made this visible. In her strange situation studies, infants protested separation, then settled quickly when reunited with a caregiver whose presence felt coherent. Reunion with a caregiver who looked calm but carried tension produced slower recovery. The difference was not in the script of words, it was in physiological alignment.

Modern measures confirm what Bowlby and Ainsworth intuited. Heart-rate variability, a marker of nervous system flexibility, rises when adult and child are attuned. When the adult’s calm is masked rather than real, synchrony falters. The child’s body stays on alert, even as the adult speaks gently. Biology listens to congruence, not performance.

Stephen Porges’ work helps explain the mechanism. The vagus nerve monitors micro-expressions, vocal prosody, and breath patterns for cues of safety. A voice that sounds smooth but carries strain still registers as risk. A smile that hides frustration subtly tightens facial muscles, and the child’s system notices. Defence begins before the sentence ends.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
This is why logic often fails in heated moments. When a child is dysregulated, their hearing narrows to the music of your voice rather than the meaning of your words. Reason returns only when the music changes. Soft eyes, slower breath, a posture that says I am here... these open the channel that advice cannot reach on its own.

Jean Liedloff, observing the Yequanna, saw that adults did not manage children into calm, they lived calm as a baseline. Doing and being were not split into separate roles. Work, conversation, and care flowed together. Children grew up decoding authenticity, not navigating contradictions between face and feeling. Trust emerged because nothing needed translation.

Co-regulation is the practical name for this ancient dance. Your settled presence lends stability to your child’s unsettled state. Boundaries still matter, yet they land differently when carried by warmth. A firm limit spoken from ease closes the argument without closing the relationship. The lesson arrives through feeling first, then words.

Transparency strengthens this effect. When you say, I am irritated, and I am staying, the signals match. The child relaxes because reality is now consistent. They learn that difficult emotions can be held without rupture. Over time, both of you develop the capacity to remain connected while truth is told.

This is the heart of embodied leadership at home. You do not need perfect composure; you need honest coherence. The body that tells the truth becomes the environment in which a child’s biology can rest. From that rest, curiosity returns, play returns, and guidance can finally be heard.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Authenticity is not indulgence; it is biology’s requirement for trust. When your inner world and your outer signals align, a child’s system relaxes automatically. The tone of voice, the micro-movements of the face, even the pauses between words transmit coherence. Regulation is not taught, it is borrowed. A nervous system calms by feeling another that is already calm.

Daniel Siegel describes this as “mindsight”—the ability to perceive the mind behind behaviour. When parents stay aware of their own state, they stop reacting and start relating. You are no longer a manager of conduct but a mirror of integration. The child’s scattered energy meets your steadiness and slowly organises itself around it.

As we have already seen in Bowlby’s work, secure attachment depends on consistency, not perfection. A parent who loses temper yet repairs openly offers a stronger model than one who never appears to falter. The first teaches resilience; the second teaches performance. Resilience grows from seeing that rupture can be mended without shame.

Authenticity therefore requires humility. It means saying, “I was tense,” or, “That frightened me,” without collapsing into guilt. Such admissions dissolve the static between you. The child experiences reality restored: signals now match, and safety returns. In that moment both nervous systems exhale.

Culture still confuses authenticity with oversharing, but the two are different. Oversharing burdens the child; authenticity steadies them. You do not hand your child your feelings to carry; you show them that you can carry your own while staying present. It is the difference between spilling emotion and inhabiting it consciously.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Stephen Porges reminds us that safety is contagious. When the vagus nerve detects ease, it invites rest and connection. This contagion works in both directions. If you hide anxiety, the child catches it; if you reveal coherence, they catch that instead. Your state is the blueprint they use to design their own.

Authenticity also frees you from self-surveillance. Pretence consumes energy; presence restores it. When you stop acting calm and start being honest, attention flows outward instead of inward. You hear more, see more, and can respond with precision rather than with performance. Energy once spent on suppression becomes available for empathy.

The effect accumulates. Days feel less brittle, evenings quieter. Misbehaviour no longer feels like a threat to authority but as information about connection. A house that once echoed with correction begins to hum with conversation. Discipline softens into guidance because everyone can afford to listen.

Jean Liedloff observed among the Yequanna that adults seldom explained feelings; they embodied them. Their authenticity required no vocabulary. Children read the cues and adjusted naturally. Modern families can reclaim that simplicity. We do not need perfect communication; we need embodied honesty.

Authenticity does not eliminate conflict. It changes the atmosphere in which conflict occurs. When truth replaces pretence, emotion becomes safe to touch. Anger loses its sharpness, sadness finds witness, and love resumes its ordinary shape. The nervous system knows when it is home.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Perfect, Geoffrey — that’s exactly the rhythm we’ll keep: one fully realised section at a time, always ten paragraphs, completely aligned with the Book Magic arc, tone, and style rules we’ve established.

Here’s the final narrative movement of Chapter 3 – The Calm You Fake before we move to the closing elements.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
There comes a moment in every parent’s journey when maintaining appearances simply costs too much. You feel the ache of pretending calm, the dissonance of smiling through strain, the weariness of endless self-control. That moment, though uncomfortable, is an invitation. It signals that your body is ready to trade performance for presence. The freedom you seek begins the moment you stop needing to look calm and start allowing yourself to be real.

To be real is not to indulge every impulse or to abandon grace. It is to let truth and compassion occupy the same space. When you are honest about frustration yet remain steady, your child learns that strength and vulnerability can coexist. The myth of the unshakable parent dissolves, replaced by the image of a human being who can feel fully and stay connected. That image will teach your child far more than any perfectly executed calm ever could.

Daniel Siegel often reminds parents that integration is the hallmark of mental health. Integration means linking the differentiated parts of ourselves into a coherent whole. Calm without truth fragments; truth integrated with compassion unites. When you practice integration in front of your child, you model wholeness rather than balance. Wholeness has room for imperfection; balance demands constant adjustment.

Authenticity transforms discipline too. When a child sees that your correction comes from clarity rather than irritation, they listen differently. The instruction lands as guidance, not control. The respect they feel does not arise from fear but from recognition. They sense the difference between authority that performs power and authority that embodies it. The latter anchors; the former unsettles.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Over time, the need to appear calm fades. You find that being honest costs less energy than managing impressions. Fatigue lifts. The house grows quieter, not because emotions vanish, but because truth travels faster than misunderstanding. Children raised in this clarity do not fear emotion; they trust it. They grow up knowing that intensity does not mean danger, and that repair is always possible.

The Yequanna embodied this freedom. They did not label emotions as good or bad; they treated them as weather passing through. A storm was met with shelter, not shame. Adults allowed feelings to move naturally until balance returned. In modern homes, this can sound idealistic, yet the principle remains timeless: emotions regulate when they are permitted to complete their cycle in the presence of connection.

The freedom of being real extends beyond the family. It changes how you meet the world. You stop striving to appear composed in conversations, at work, or in friendship. Others sense the same safety your child feels. Honesty attracts ease; pretence repels it. Gradually, authenticity becomes the default rather than the exception. Calm ceases to be a goal; it becomes a by-product of alignment.

This is the quiet revolution parenting was meant to spark. Every time an adult chooses coherence over control, they rewrite the emotional DNA of their lineage. Future generations inherit less anxiety and more trust. The home becomes a microcosm of peace, not because life is easier, but because truth is welcome there.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
To live this way requires compassion for yourself. There will still be days when the old performance creeps in. You will catch yourself trying to manage tone or suppress irritation. When that happens, remember: awareness is already the return. The moment you notice, you are back. You cannot fail at being real; you can only forget and remember again.

Freedom is not the absence of emotion; it is the absence of disguise. When love and truth meet without competition, calm no longer needs to be faked. It arises naturally, the way breath follows stillness and stillness follows trust. That is the calm your child has been waiting to feel all along.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Where in your parenting do you notice yourself performing calm instead of feeling it?
What happens in your body when you hide frustration behind a gentle voice?
Can you recall a moment when honesty brought more peace than restraint?
How does your child respond when your tone matches your emotion rather than masking it?
What would change if you believed that authenticity was safer than perfection?
How might your home sound if everyone was allowed to be real?
What would calm mean if it no longer required pretending?

True calm begins the moment you stop trying to look peaceful and start trusting that love can hold the truth.

You may already sense that the story of modern parenting did not appear by chance. The ideals of composure, control, and performance were inherited from a culture that once valued order above relationship. In the next chapter we will step back in time to see how that story was built, how behaviourism and industry reshaped family life, and why remembering the past becomes the key to finding ease in the present and changing the future.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
section: The Invention of the Modern Parent (part 1)
text:
Parenting has not always looked the way it does today. For most of human history, children were raised within a flow of daily life, folded into the rhythm of work, story, and community. No one spoke of “parenting” as a separate activity. Guidance happened through proximity and imitation. Adults carried infants on their backs, spoke in ordinary tones, and expected maturity to emerge naturally with time. The word parenting itself did not exist until the mid-twentieth century, when raising children became an enterprise to be managed.

The shift began with industrialisation. Families left extended kin networks for city life, and the collective wisdom that once held them scattered. Work moved away from the home, separating adults from their children for long hours. The factory clock replaced the rhythm of daylight, and predictability became the new virtue. When people learned to value precision and control in production, they began to apply the same ideals to family life. The child became a small project, and the parent a manager.

This industrial inheritance created the first generation of what we might call the modern parent—task-oriented, efficient, and outcome-driven. Success meant discipline; failure meant disorder. The unpredictability of childhood no longer looked like growth but like error. It is no coincidence that the first “parenting manuals” appeared during this period, mirroring the instruction sheets of machinery. They promised smoother running, fewer breakdowns, more compliant results.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
section: The Invention of the Modern Parent (part 2)
text:
Behavioural science soon offered intellectual reinforcement. In the early 1900s, psychologists such as John B. Watson claimed that affection could spoil children and that independence must be trained early. In his book Psychological Care of Infant and Child, Watson warned against hugging or kissing one’s offspring, urging parents to shake hands with them instead. His tone was scientific, but his message was moral: emotion was inefficiency. The good parent was rational, composed, and detached enough to maintain control.

By the middle of the century, B. F. Skinner expanded these ideas into a complete system of behavioural conditioning. Through reward and consequence, behaviour could be engineered. Schools adopted the model, governments funded its research, and the approach soon seeped into homes. Parents learned that outcomes were measurable, that love could be structured, and that obedience was the proof of success. Few noticed how far this was from the biological blueprint of relationship.

Women, newly entering the workforce, carried an additional layer of pressure. They were expected to perform productivity in both spheres, efficient employee by day, perfect parent by night. Sociologist Arlie Hochschild would later describe this as the “second shift,” a cycle of unending labour disguised as devotion. Fathers too were drawn into performance, equating authority with distance, involvement with control. The emotional village that once shared responsibility had become a private stage.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
section: The Invention of the Modern Parent (part 3)
text:
Technology accelerated the separation. The rise of radio and later television turned expert advice into entertainment. Gurus replaced elders; theories replaced stories. Parents learned to distrust their instincts, seeking validation from voices that promised certainty. The self that once trusted the quiet wisdom of connection now looked outward for permission to be itself.

By the 1970s and 80s, the language of management dominated every corner of life. Productivity seminars and child-rearing books spoke the same dialect: measurable goals, consistent feedback, continuous improvement. What began as the dream of efficiency in factories had become the framework for love at home. The parent no longer belonged to a lineage of nurturers but to a profession of performers.

This transformation did not happen maliciously; it happened logically. Each generation wanted to do better, to apply reason to chaos, to create security in uncertain times. Yet the more we tried to engineer childhood, the less natural it became. The spontaneity that once shaped learning turned into curricula, and the mystery of growth became a problem to solve. The modern parent was invented out of good intentions, yet those same intentions quietly erased the instincts that made parenting human.

If you trace the exhaustion of today’s parents back through history, you find its roots here, in the moment when love was recast as labour and presence was replaced by productivity. To understand how to return to ease, we must first see how the idea of control became confused with care. That story continues in the next section, where we look at the rise of behaviourism and how its promise of order reshaped our understanding of relationship.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
section: Behaviourism and the Age of Control (part 1)
text:
At the dawn of the twentieth century, psychology began to reimagine itself as a science of prediction. The messy mysteries of the mind were replaced with measurable behaviour. John B. Watson declared that given control of the environment, he could turn any infant into any type of adult be it doctor, lawyer, or thief. His claim was seductive. It promised mastery over uncertainty, a cure for the chaos of emotion. Behaviourism became the architecture of modern control.

Watson’s ideas were born into a culture that already revered efficiency. Industry had taught society that anything could be improved through repetition and discipline. Human life, once guided by stories and rituals, was recast as a series of problems to be solved. The new psychology fit perfectly. It offered techniques for shaping conduct, and parents eagerly applied them at home. The nursery became a laboratory for progress.

B. F. Skinner expanded this experiment into an entire worldview. His famous boxes, in which pigeons learned to press levers for food, became metaphors for human life. The message was clear: behaviour could be engineered through reinforcement. Praise replaced proximity; reward replaced relationship. Children learned to respond to approval rather than attunement. The human heart was quietly rewired to serve predictability.

In its early decades, behaviourism appeared humane compared with the harsh physical punishments it replaced. Consequences and rewards felt enlightened, even compassionate. Yet beneath the surface was a subtle shift: love began to depend on performance. Approval became conditional. A smile or a star meant belonging; disapproval meant withdrawal. The nervous system learned to chase acceptance instead of connection.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
section: Behaviourism and the Age of Control (part 2)
text:
John Bowlby, whose attachment theory we met earlier, challenged this paradigm. Drawing on his work with war orphans, he saw that human development thrived not on conditioning but on closeness. He argued that separation anxiety was not a flaw to extinguish but a sign of healthy dependence. His research suggested that children deprived of warmth did not become disciplined—they became detached. Still, the culture was slow to listen. Control remained the more fashionable form of love.

Schools institutionalised the behavioural model. Bells, grades, and punishments trained children to obey the clock. Obedience was mistaken for maturity, compliance for respect. The logic was self-reinforcing: if rewards worked, use more; if they failed, tighten control. Few questioned whether the system itself was misaligned with biology. By the time these children became parents, they had internalised the same structure. They raised their own children in a feedback loop of conditioned affection.

Stephen Porges’ later discoveries reveal why behaviourism could never deliver what it promised. The nervous system does not respond predictably to external control; it responds to perceived safety. A child’s compliance under threat is not learning—it is survival. Fear may create short-term obedience, but it silences curiosity. The body remembers the cost of suppression long after the behaviour has improved.

The rise of consumer culture deepened this conditioning. Advertising borrowed directly from behavioural science, using reward cues to shape desire. Families began to operate on the same principle. Every good deed earned a treat, every misstep a withdrawal. Childhood became a marketplace of incentives, and parents became reluctant merchants of motivation. Connection thinned to a transaction.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
section: Behaviourism and the Age of Control (part 3)
text:
By the late twentieth century, a generation had grown up fluent in control but starved of ease. The promise of behaviourism—predictable outcomes through effort—had not made families calmer. It had made them anxious. When the system failed, parents blamed themselves rather than the model. They assumed they had not applied the technique hard enough. Few realised that the very premise was flawed: love cannot be engineered.

Behaviourism did teach the world one lasting truth, though it did not mean to. It proved that environment shapes behaviour. What it missed is that relationship is the deepest environment of all. When connection is secure, guidance flows naturally; when it is broken, no technique will hold. The age of control left us skilled at managing behaviour but unsure how to nurture being. Relearning that is the work ahead.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
section: The Lost Village and the Rise of the Expert (part 1)
text:
For most of human history, parents were never alone. The responsibility of raising children was spread across generations and woven into the daily rhythm of community life. Grandparents carried babies, older siblings taught younger ones, neighbours offered guidance without presumption. Each adult contributed a thread to the child’s sense of belonging. This shared care created balance: no single person had to be perfect, because the whole group held the child steady.

Industrialisation quietly dismantled that village. Families moved for work, leaving extended kin networks behind. The home became a private unit rather than a communal space. What was once a collective task became an individual responsibility. Without elders or peers to lean on, parents turned inward, measuring themselves against impossible standards. Isolation became the invisible inheritance of modern love.

As social bonds thinned, a new figure stepped into the gap: the expert. Psychologists, paediatricians, and education theorists became the new elders of the industrial age. Their guidance was well-intentioned, yet it carried a subtle message; that parents could not be trusted to know. The wisdom once drawn from lived experience was replaced by instructions from outside the family circle. Love became a subject of study rather than a shared instinct.

The first wave of experts offered reassurance in the language of authority. They provided structure, charts, and schedules that soothed parental anxiety. Mothers who doubted their intuition could now consult the pages of Dr Spock or the lectures of Watson. Fathers found manuals that defined discipline as leadership. Each new book promised to solve the uncertainty that community once absorbed through conversation. Parents obeyed the written word as they had once obeyed the rhythm of the group.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
section: The Lost Village and the Rise of the Expert (part 2)
text:
Yet every prescription came with an unintended side effect. The more parents followed external rules, the less they trusted their own perception. Doubt grew where instinct once lived. The simple act of holding a crying baby became a moral dilemma rather than a moment of connection. Guidance, once shared horizontally among peers, now descended vertically from professionals. Authority moved from relationship to reference.

Sociologist Ivan Illich warned of this phenomenon in the 1970s, calling it the “professionalisation of life.” He argued that expertise, while useful, often replaces local wisdom instead of enriching it. People forget how to do for themselves what institutions promise to do better. Parenting followed that pattern. The intimate knowledge of children’s rhythms, honed over millennia, was displaced by standardised advice. The result was a population of parents both educated and uncertain.

Technology amplified this dependence. In the late twentieth century, television and magazines brought experts into every living room. Advice became entertainment, and entertainment became the new community. Instead of gathering at a neighbour’s table, parents gathered around screens. Each new decade produced its fashionable theories: strict routines, attachment parenting, positive discipline. The language changed, but the message stayed the same; someone out there knows better than you.

Jean Liedloff, writing about the Yequanna, offered a quiet counterpoint. Among them, advice was unnecessary because life itself was the teacher. Children were included in adult activity, not analysed from afar. Their needs were met through constant presence, not consultation. Modern parents, she noted, had lost this natural confidence. They sought instruction on how to do what their ancestors had done intuitively: love without agenda.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
section: The Lost Village and the Rise of the Expert (part 3)
text:
The loss of the village is not only physical; it is psychological. When parents internalise the voice of the expert, they silence their own. Each decision becomes a test of competence rather than an act of relationship. The anxiety that results is often misread as personal failure, when in truth it is cultural displacement. We were designed to raise children together, not in isolation and comparison.

Rebuilding that sense of collective wisdom begins with trust. Trust in your own perception, trust in shared humanity, trust that presence is still the most sophisticated tool ever invented for raising a child. The experts can inform, but they cannot replace the felt intelligence that arises from connection. The village may have been lost, but the capacity to create one again lives quietly inside every act of genuine listening.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
By the end of the twentieth century, the story of control had begun to wear thin. Parents were exhausted, children anxious, families busier yet lonelier than ever. The expert advice that once promised certainty started to sound like another list of demands. Beneath the surface of progress, an older truth stirred: human beings are not problems to be managed; they are relationships to be felt.

The turning point did not arrive as revolution but as fatigue. Parents reached the limit of constant doing and perfect composure. The search for the right method had produced too many. Techniques contradicted each other, theories shifted with fashion, and the only constant was pressure. Somewhere between the schedules and the screens, people began to wonder if something essential had been misplaced.

At the same time, new sciences began to echo what ancient wisdom had never forgotten. Attachment theory matured, neuroscience revealed the social brain, and developmental psychology rediscovered the body’s role in emotion. Stephen Porges’ polyvagal research, Daniel Siegel’s work on integration, and Bruce Perry’s studies of trauma all pointed to the same conclusion: relationship is regulation. Safety cannot be taught; it must be felt.

This convergence created a quiet rebellion against the age of control. Parents began to question not only the advice but the assumptions beneath it. What if obedience was never the goal? What if cooperation emerged naturally when children felt secure? These questions signalled a return to curiosity, the quality behaviourism had most successfully suppressed.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
The information era accelerated awareness even as it deepened confusion. Access to research gave parents language for what they already sensed. Words like co-regulation, nervous system, and emotional safety entered everyday conversation. Yet without lived experience to anchor them, the new knowledge sometimes became another performance. People spoke the language of connection while still inhabiting the mindset of control.

The next evolution required embodiment, not theory. The truth that science described had to be felt again in ordinary life. It began in small moments: a parent choosing to pause instead of lecture, to listen instead of explain. These shifts seemed minor, but they carried revolutionary weight. They marked the transition from control to alignment.

Jean Liedloff’s reflections on the Yequanna remained a touchstone for many. She had seen what modernity forgot: that children thrive in the presence of adults who trust themselves. Her work, once dismissed as romantic, found new resonance among those weary of constant intervention. The Yequanna were not idealised; they were simply whole. Their coherence became a mirror for our fragmentation.

By the early twenty-first century, this restoration of wholeness began to shape a new conversation. Parents sought community again, not as a nostalgic return to the past but as a living necessity. Circles of discussion replaced hierarchies of instruction. The wisdom once lost to experts began to reappear in shared experience, in the simple act of listening without judgement.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
The turning point is still unfolding. We stand between eras: the age of management fading, the age of alignment emerging. The journey now is not toward more knowledge but toward integration—remembering that love, safety, and presence were never separate disciplines but expressions of one biological truth. What was once called instinct is simply relationship unbroken.

When we see that history has shaped our exhaustion, compassion replaces blame. We can forgive ourselves for the ways we have tried to love through control. The next chapter begins with that forgiveness, tracing how the remnants of these old systems live in our daily reactions and how recognising them becomes the first act of freedom.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Which parts of your parenting feel inherited rather than chosen?
When do you notice the voice of the expert replacing your own?
How has the loss of community shaped the way you measure yourself?
What patterns of control still echo through your family story?
Where might trust in your natural instincts begin to return?
What does “village” mean for you now, and how could you rebuild it?
If love no longer needed to prove itself through control, what would it sound like in your home?

We cannot heal the present until we understand the story that built it.

The age of control gave us structure but stole our ease. To move beyond it, we must first see how deeply it lives within us. In the next chapter we turn inward, exploring the reflex to manage, to direct, and to perfect. We will see how this mirror of control hides in our daily reactions and how meeting it with awareness begins the slow return to freedom.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Every generation begins with good intentions. We promise ourselves we will not repeat the mistakes of our parents. We will be calmer, kinder, more enlightened. Yet in moments of stress, the same patterns rise. Words we swore we would never say slip out; tones we thought we had outgrown return without warning. It is not failure; it is inheritance. Control is the reflex of an age, passed quietly from body to body.

You learned early that safety depended on keeping things in order. A tidy room meant peace, a silent child meant success. The lessons were subtle but constant: stay composed, stay productive, stay good. Somewhere along the way, the nervous system equated control with love. To lose control felt like losing worth. That association lingers in the body long after the mind outgrows it.

Control, at its root, is not cruelty but fear. Fear of chaos, fear of rejection, fear of not being enough. Parents who grew up under unpredictable authority often swing to the opposite extreme, mistaking rigidity for safety. The mind says, “Never again,” but the body still flinches at uncertainty. Without awareness, you recreate the very climate you are trying to escape.

Stephen Porges’ research helps us see why. The body’s threat response is ancient and automatic. When a child defies, the parent’s nervous system interprets danger, not disrespect. Heart rate rises, breath shortens, and the impulse to regain control surges. You may call it discipline, but biology knows it as self-defence. To regulate a child, you must first recognise that your body is speaking too.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
John Bowlby noted that patterns of attachment transmit across generations. The comfort or control you experienced as a child becomes the blueprint you unconsciously pass on. If love was conditional, you may seek to earn it through achievement. If emotion was unsafe, you may try to contain it in others. Each attempt to manage your child’s feeling is a conversation with your younger self.

This is why control feels so personal. When your child resists, it touches the oldest parts of you—the memories that never had space to unfold. What you interpret as defiance may simply be the echo of your own unspoken voice. The child becomes a mirror, reflecting the places in you still waiting for permission to be human.

Jean Liedloff’s account of the Yequanna contrasts this inheritance. Their adults did not try to shape children through pressure or performance. They trusted the innate movement towards maturity. Authority came through example, not enforcement. When a child protested, it was met with presence rather than panic. That confidence came from coherence; no one was acting a part.

Modern parents rarely enjoy that coherence. We live in societies that praise composure and productivity while punishing vulnerability. The result is an unspoken tension between what we feel and what we display. This tension breeds control. It is easier to manage a child’s outburst than to meet our own discomfort. So we reach for the familiar reflex, trying to fix externally what aches internally.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Awareness transforms this pattern. When you notice the urge to control, pause long enough to ask what it is protecting. Often it is the child you once were, still seeking reassurance. Meeting that part with compassion dissolves the reflex. The need to command softens into curiosity. Control fades not through technique but through tenderness toward your own story.

The inherited reflex loses power when it is seen. Each time you choose to notice rather than react, you rewrite the script for the next generation. Your child learns that authority and empathy can coexist, that power can be gentle. Control was never the problem; unconscious control was. Awareness turns inheritance into evolution.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Every reaction has a history. The flash of anger, the tightening of the jaw, the impulse to correct, all arise before thought has a chance to intervene. The mind narrates the event, but the body has already acted. To understand control, we must look beneath behaviour to the systems that drive it. Reaction is not a moral choice; it is a physiological pattern repeating itself until it is met with awareness.

When a child’s behaviour challenges you, the first thing that changes is breath. The inhale shortens, the exhale stalls. Muscles prepare for defence, pupils widen, tone hardens. This is biology, not bad parenting. The nervous system registers threat long before logic catches up. What looks like impatience is often the body’s plea for safety.

Stephen Porges’ polyvagal theory maps this beautifully. The body’s hierarchy of responses moves from social engagement to fight or flight, and finally to collapse. When you are calm, you can connect and reason. When stress rises, you slip into protection. The transition happens silently within milliseconds. Unless recognised, it governs every interaction. The urge to control is often the fight reflex wearing the mask of authority.

Knowing this changes everything. Instead of judging yourself for losing patience, you can recognise that your biology has shifted state. The task is no longer to suppress reaction but to translate it. A deep breath, a softer tone, a momentary pause resets the system. Regulation begins in the body, not the mind. You cannot think your way into calm; you must feel your way there.
