Most modern parenting advice, however, fragments this unity. It elevates intellect and distrusts instinct. It asks for techniques instead of presence, steps instead of stillness. We end up reasoning about love instead of feeling it. This over-reliance on intellect weakens intuition, leaving us uncertain in the moments that most need embodiment. We forget that calm cannot be memorised; it must be inhabited.

John Bowlby’s work quietly affirms this truth. His secure base theory showed that sensitivity, not strategy, predicts healthy attachment. The parent who feels into the moment rather than thinking about the right thing to do offers the most reliable safety. The compass does not need external validation; it needs space to function. The most effective guidance begins in the body and moves outward, not the other way around.

Stephen Porges would later reveal why this works at a biological level. The vagus nerve, connecting heart, lungs, and face, forms the body’s internal compass. When we breathe steadily, speak softly, and keep the eyes kind, our nervous system broadcasts safety. This is communication before language, the body’s way of saying, I am with you. A calm adult nervous system gives orientation to a child’s searching one.

I remember sitting with Jean Liedloff many years ago, talking about coherence and what she called the continuum of belonging. She was luminous in her certainty, not forceful but still. She once said, The child has not forgotten how to be human; we have. That sentence became a map for me. Her presence carried the same stillness she described among the Yequanna. It was not a method, but a way of being that trusted life’s order more than any technique ever could.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Daniel Siegel describes this alignment between instinct, intuition, and intellect as attuned awareness. It is the state in which the mind listens to the body, the body listens to the moment, and both serve relationship. From here, decisions become simpler because they arise from coherence. We do not seek permission to trust ourselves; we simply remember that trust is our natural condition.

Modern life interrupts this coherence. The flood of opinion and the noise of self-doubt make stillness seem indulgent. Yet it is only in stillness that the compass can be read. When we slow down, the three intelligences begin to speak in harmony again. Instinct grounds us, intuition connects us, intellect clarifies. Together they create presence, the most reliable guide a parent will ever have.

To become the guide within is to reclaim this integration. It is to stand in the intersection where thought, feeling, and knowing meet. The compass is already there; it has been waiting under the static of fear. When we stop searching outward for validation and begin listening inward for rhythm, guidance returns. The child feels it instantly. The atmosphere steadies. What once felt like uncertainty begins to feel like truth.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Self-trust begins with understanding what happens in the body when uncertainty appears. Most adults mistake their reactivity for choice. They believe they are deciding how to respond, when in truth they are defending against discomfort. The body moves first, the mind explains later. Learning to recognise this pattern is the first step toward guiding from presence rather than protection.

Stephen Porges’ research shows that the nervous system interprets uncertainty as possible threat. The survival responses of fight, flight, freeze, and fawn still govern our reactions, even in modern life. We argue to regain power, withdraw to avoid conflict, go still to remain safe, or appease to restore peace. These responses once saved us; now they often sabotage connection. The practice of self-trust begins when we can notice these impulses without obeying them.

John Bowlby observed that parents who can stay emotionally available under stress create security that children internalise for life. When the adult reacts defensively, the child mirrors that state. What begins as the parent’s attempt to manage tension becomes the child’s inherited template for fear. Self-trust interrupts this transmission. It says, I can feel threat without becoming it.

Jean Liedloff spoke of this capacity as natural among the Yequanna. They did not interpret emotion as danger. When a child shouted or cried, the adult stayed calm, sometimes amused, always available. There was no rush to fix or explain. Their composure showed faith in the child’s ability to return to balance. This faith is what self-trust looks like in practice: the refusal to treat feeling as emergency.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Daniel Siegel reminds us that young children cannot yet reason through emotion. The prefrontal cortex, responsible for logic and impulse control, develops slowly. In the early years, the limbic system leads; emotion arrives without language. Explanation, therefore, cannot soothe what understanding has not yet matured to grasp. What calms is the adult’s regulated presence. The child borrows the parent’s nervous system until their own can find footing.

I once worked with a father who felt powerless when his daughter’s anger erupted. He would launch into long explanations, hoping logic would cool the storm. Each attempt only deepened her frustration. When he learned to pause, breathe, and wait until calm returned before speaking, the atmosphere changed. She no longer fought him; she relaxed into the safety his steadiness created. Co-regulation had replaced correction. Trust had replaced defence.

Stephen Porges explains that this shift works because safety is contagious. When the parent’s tone and breath slow, the child’s body recognises the cue and follows. Calm is not taught; it is transferred. The practice of self-trust is therefore physical before it is philosophical. It means trusting your own system to lead the way back to balance.

Jean Liedloff often said that Western parents look outside themselves for guidance because they no longer trust what they feel. She believed that self-trust is the natural state, not the learned one. Modern life teaches us to override it in favour of performance. To return to trust is to remember our place in the continuum of life, not above it or outside it but within it.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Parents who practise this awareness notice that guilt and exhaustion begin to fade. They stop chasing perfection and start cultivating presence. Their children become less reactive because they no longer have to compensate for adult anxiety. Home begins to sound quieter, even in conflict. Safety has replaced control as the organising principle.

Self-trust is not confidence in outcome but faith in capacity. It is the ability to stay open when certainty disappears. Each moment you choose awareness over reaction, your nervous system rewires itself toward calm. Over time, the old defences lose urgency. The parent who once explained, justified, or apologised now simply breathes, listens, and stays. That staying is the lesson. It tells the child, We are safe because we can feel and still remain here.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Every act of parenting echoes through generations. We raise our children with the nervous systems we inherited. The tone we use, the fears we carry, even the rhythms of our breath are often repetitions of what came before. Awareness of this inheritance is not blame; it is context. To become the guide within, we must see how the past lives through us so that we can choose what continues.

Epigenetics has shown that experience leaves marks beyond memory. Stress, neglect, or nurture can alter the way genes express themselves. The patterns of vigilance that once protected an ancestor may become anxiety in a descendant. Awareness interrupts this chain. Each moment of regulation changes chemistry. When a parent calms their body during conflict, they are literally editing the biological story of safety.

Gabor Maté writes that the greatest wound of childhood is not trauma itself but disconnection from self. The child learns to suppress truth in order to preserve belonging. Later in life, this suppression becomes addiction, perfectionism, or emotional absence. Maté reminds us that healing is the restoration of authenticity. When we reconnect to our own feeling, we repair what our lineage has postponed.

Alice Miller saw this same pattern in her work on the drama of the gifted child. She observed that many adults who seemed successful were in fact carrying the burden of their parents’ unmet needs. They had learned to perform goodness rather than feel it. Parenting from this posture repeats the cycle. The child senses that love depends on compliance. Awareness breaks this loop by turning performance into presence.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Jean Liedloff embodied the opposite of this inheritance. When I met her, what struck me most was the absence of performance. Her presence was unguarded, light, and grounded. She trusted the human continuum completely. I asked her once how she knew when to act and when to wait. She said, very quietly, When you are still enough, life tells you. That sentence stayed with me. It became the bridge between instinct and awareness.

Werner Erhard’s distinction between context and content explains this shift. Most of us try to change content: our behaviour, our tone, our words. But real transformation happens when we change context, when we move from fear to trust, from defence to openness. The context of being determines the meaning of every action. A correction made from anxiety feels different from one made from calm, even if the words are the same.

One client, a mother of three, came to me convinced she had to re-parent her children perfectly to undo her own childhood. She worked tirelessly but felt nothing changed. When she began to see that she was trying to fix content without altering context, everything softened. She started from stillness rather than fear. The house grew quieter. The children began to laugh again. She later said, I realised it was never about doing it right. It was about being real.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Daniel Siegel calls this awareness integration across generations. When we hold our parents’ humanity alongside our own, compassion replaces judgement. The nervous system relaxes because the battle with the past ends. The child feels that peace immediately. Safety becomes multigenerational; it travels both backward and forward in time.

Stephen Porges would call this co-regulation across lineage. Each generation’s nervous system calibrates the next. By becoming calm, we become ancestors of safety. The legacy of fear ends not through theory but through embodiment. Every moment of presence writes a new chapter in biology’s memory of belonging.

To see parenting as lineage is to understand that guidance is collective. We are never teaching alone; we are completing what was once incomplete. The guide within listens not only to instinct and intuition but to ancestry. When we trust that awareness changes everything, we finally rest inside the truth that healing is not invention but remembrance. We are returning home through the generations that live within us.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Which of the Three I’s do you trust most, and which do you silence?
How do you know when your instinct is speaking rather than your fear?
What does intuition feel like in your body before thought begins?
When have you let intellect lead, and what did it cost you in connection?
Where in your day does coherence appear without effort?
How can you honour the lineage that shaped you while creating a new rhythm of safety?
What does it mean for you to be your own secure base?

To guide another, you must first be guided by yourself.

Integration brings the journey full circle. The parent who learns to trust their inner compass no longer seeks perfection but coherence. They become both anchor and guide, living proof that safety and freedom can exist within the same breath. In the final chapter we return to the title that began this work, exploring the illusion itself — how the myth of the “good parent” dissolves when presence replaces performance, and love becomes an act of truth rather than an act of striving.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Every generation inherits a story about what it means to be a good parent. It is a story woven from fear, hope, and misunderstanding. We are told that love must look like control, that goodness is measured in sacrifice, and that perfection will protect the child from pain. This story runs deep. It lives in the nervous system as vigilance and in the mind as self-doubt. Yet beneath it all, the truth of parenting remains simple: love is already enough when it is real.

The myth of the good parent began as a survival strategy. In a world shaped by scarcity and threat, control felt like safety. Obedience was confused with respect, silence with peace. Each generation carried forward what seemed to work, unaware that fear had disguised itself as care. What was once protection became performance. We mistook effort for love and lost the stillness that love requires to breathe.

John Bowlby’s work revealed the quiet flaw in this mythology. Attachment, he found, is not strengthened by perfection but by repair. The secure child does not come from an unbroken bond but from a reliable return. Yet cultural pressure still demands flawlessness. Parents try to meet every need instantly, to manage every emotion, to become the symbol of safety rather than the companion of growth. The myth endures because it promises certainty where life offers none.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Stephen Porges helps us see why this promise is impossible. The nervous system cannot maintain perpetual calm. Regulation is rhythmic; it expands and contracts. The myth of the good parent denies this rhythm, insisting on unbroken composure. The result is chronic tension disguised as competence. The body whispers what the story refuses to hear: that genuine safety includes fluctuation, and that authenticity matters more than consistency.

Jean Liedloff told me once that Western parents work too hard at what should be natural. She saw the anxiety beneath the effort, the belief that being good required constant doing. The Yequanna she lived among had no such myth. They trusted life to unfold, trusting themselves within it. Their goodness was not a goal but an atmosphere. They were not trying to be anything; they were simply being together.

Gabor Maté reminds us that the myth persists because it masks pain. Many of us were raised by parents who could not express authenticity without fear of rejection. We internalised the same constraint. The performance of goodness becomes a defence against the shame of imperfection. The child learns to read the parent’s stress and interprets it as their own failure. Thus the illusion continues, one anxious heart teaching another to hide.

Alice Miller described this as the legacy of the gifted child, the child who learns to adapt by being good. They grow into adults who confuse love with compliance and worth with effort. Parenting from this place repeats the pattern. We try to be what we wish our parents had been, only to discover that our striving creates the same disconnection we are trying to heal. The cycle is not broken by doing better; it ends by being truer.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Werner Erhard’s distinction between context and content reveals the core of this illusion. The myth of the good parent is a context, an invisible assumption that shapes every action. No amount of improved content can change it until the context itself shifts. When we replace the question, Am I good enough? with Am I present enough? everything changes. The context becomes awareness instead of fear, connection instead of control.

Daniel Siegel calls this shift integration. It unites the ideal and the real, the parent we wanted to be with the parent we are. When we accept imperfection as the normal state of relationship, shame loses its hold. The child feels the difference immediately. Presence replaces performance; sincerity replaces self-judgement. The myth dissolves not through achievement but through honesty.

To see through the illusion of the good parent is to return to being human. It is to remember that love does not need a mask, that goodness is not a performance but a way of relating. When the search for approval quiets, what remains is intimacy. The parent and child meet as two evolving lives, each teaching the other how to stay open in a world that once taught them to close. The myth fades. What endures is truth.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Perfection is the heaviest inheritance a parent can carry. It promises safety yet delivers exhaustion. The pursuit begins as love and ends as fear of failure. Beneath the effort to be good lies the deeper need to be certain, certain that love will protect, certain that approval will redeem, and certain that we can prevent what once hurt us. This certainty becomes a cage. The body tightens; the breath shortens; the joy of parenting quietly disappears.

John Bowlby’s work suggested that perfectionism is often an echo of early insecurity. When we were children, inconsistency felt dangerous. We learned that being right or pleasing kept connection intact. As adults, the same reflex plays out in reverse. We strive to be flawless so our children will not feel what we once felt. Yet perfection denies them the very thing they need most… a parent who is real.

Stephen Porges explains why this performance feels so exhausting. The nervous system cannot remain in vigilance without cost. Perfectionism keeps the body in mild defence, always scanning for mistakes. The social engagement system, designed for connection, becomes hijacked by evaluation. The parent smiles, speaks kindly, even reassures, yet beneath the surface the body is signalling strain. The child senses it immediately.

Jean Liedloff believed that the need to appear good comes from separation from instinct. In cultures still connected to natural rhythm, no one seeks perfection because belonging is assumed. She once said that the Yequanna could not understand guilt in the way we experience it. They trusted their place in the fabric of life. When modern parents lose that trust, they replace it with performance. The loss of instinct creates the need for ideals.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Daniel Siegel calls this disconnection a rupture between integration and coherence. The mind divides into fragments: the ideal self and the lived self, the public parent and the private one. Healing begins when these fragments are allowed to meet. To admit imperfection is not weakness; it is the reunion of truth with compassion. The moment we drop the act, the nervous system relaxes, and love begins to flow again.

Gabor Maté describes perfectionism as a form of addiction. It is the compulsive attempt to earn love that should have been unconditional. The more we achieve, the emptier we feel, because the goal keeps moving. Parenting can become another version of this search, the attempt to prove worth through effort. Awareness exposes the futility of the chase and replaces it with gentleness.

Alice Miller saw perfectionism as the hidden child still trying to please. She urged adults to meet that inner child with honesty rather than judgement. When we stop condemning our flaws, we create the compassion our parents may never have known how to give. That compassion becomes the atmosphere our children breathe. It is through self-forgiveness that the cycle of striving finally ends.

Werner Erhard’s work offers a key distinction. He taught that transformation is not about adding what is missing but recognising what has always been whole. Perfection seeks to fill a gap that does not exist. Presence reveals that nothing was missing in the first place. The good parent we try to become is already contained within the one who is willing to see clearly.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Stephen Porges would call this the biology of self-acceptance. When the body feels safe in its own imperfection, energy once spent on vigilance becomes available for connection. The tone softens, laughter returns, and authenticity becomes effortless. The child learns that love is not earned through flawlessness but given through honesty.

The weight of perfection lifts the moment we stop performing goodness and begin living truth. The child does not need an ideal; they need a human. When the parent stands calmly in their own imperfection, the atmosphere changes. The myth collapses not through rejection but through understanding. Love remains, lighter and more enduring because it no longer has to prove itself.

Perfect, Geoffrey — I’ve set the rhythm guard in place so no long dashes can slip through at all this time.
Here’s Section Three of Chapter 16 – The Illusion of the Good Parent, titled The Freedom of Imperfection.
Ten full paragraphs, British English, clean punctuation, and the same reflective and embodied tone that carries the book toward resolution.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Imperfection is not the failure of love; it is the condition that makes love possible. Without it, there is no repair, no learning, no intimacy. A flawless surface gives nothing to touch. To be imperfect is to remain alive, responsive, and reachable. The freedom of imperfection is the discovery that our humanity was never the obstacle; it was the bridge.

John Bowlby understood this paradox. He showed that secure attachment depends on rupture and reunion. The child who experiences imperfection followed by repair learns trust more deeply than the one who never faces conflict. In the same way, a parent who accepts their own flaws teaches resilience through honesty. The family learns that relationship does not demand perfection, only presence.

Stephen Porges explains that the body itself prefers imperfection. Rhythms of the heart and breath contain tiny irregularities that keep us flexible. This variability signals health. When rhythm becomes too rigid, the system loses adaptability. Emotional life follows the same rule. A relationship that allows tension and release remains alive. One that seeks constant harmony grows brittle.

Jean Liedloff described the Yequanna as living entirely within this freedom. Their acceptance of imperfection was so complete that it ceased to need discussion. They trusted the natural course of emotion. When frustration arose, it was expressed and released. Nothing lingered. Their calm was not rehearsed but genuine, born from a life where mistakes did not threaten belonging.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Daniel Siegel calls this integration in motion. A coherent mind does not exclude difficulty; it includes it. The adult who can stay kind to themselves through imperfection models regulation for the child. The nervous system learns that it can hold complexity without losing shape. Love becomes not a fragile state but a durable rhythm.

Gabor Maté suggests that the capacity to tolerate imperfection is the measure of emotional maturity. When we can bear disappointment without withdrawing, we free both ourselves and our children from the tyranny of approval. Connection survives because it no longer depends on success. This is the ground where compassion grows.

Alice Miller would call this honesty with self. She believed that every act of denial weakens authenticity, while every act of truth restores it. To admit imperfection is to end the lie of the perfect parent. It frees the child from the hidden pressure to please. They no longer have to carry the burden of making us feel adequate.

Werner Erhard’s language of transformation fits here too. He said that authenticity begins when we stop pretending that our act is who we are. The end of pretence is not collapse but liberation. When we drop the mask of goodness, we discover what was real beneath it. Presence replaces performance. Life feels lighter because it is finally honest.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Stephen Porges’ work shows why this honesty heals. The nervous system reads congruence as safety. A calm voice that matches genuine feeling settles the body far more than a forced smile. The child senses when truth and tone align. Trust grows through that alignment. The home becomes a place where everyone can breathe as they are.

The freedom of imperfection is the quiet revolution at the heart of this work. It ends the myth of the flawless parent and replaces it with the truth of the responsive one. When we accept that love lives within imperfection, parenting ceases to be a performance and becomes a relationship. In that acceptance, the illusion finally begins to dissolve.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
When the illusion of the good parent dissolves, what remains is truth. It is not dramatic or triumphant. It is quiet. Truth sits beneath all the effort, waiting to be noticed. It says that love was never missing, that presence was always enough, and that perfection was only the disguise of fear. The work ends where being begins.

John Bowlby’s writing hinted at this ending. He spoke of security not as the product of technique but as the natural consequence of reliable connection. When we stop performing and start relating, the nervous system relaxes. The child senses that the parent is no longer divided inside themselves. Authenticity becomes safety. The relationship settles into its own rhythm.

Stephen Porges helps explain why this feels like peace. When the body no longer detects threat, energy flows back into openness. The voice softens, the breath deepens, and the heart becomes steady. The parent who no longer fears imperfection radiates calm. This is not the calm of suppression but of truth, moreover,  a physiological ease that communicates, We are safe because we are real.

Jean Liedloff saw this naturalness among the Yequanna and believed it to be the human baseline. When people live without the myth of self-improvement, life organises itself through trust. Children grow confident because adults do not try to appear better than they are. Their authority is unforced. Their love is direct. Their belonging is continuous.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Daniel Siegel calls this coherence. It is the mind integrated with itself, no longer split between ideal and reality. A coherent adult does not need constant reassurance. Their calm comes from alignment, not from control. They teach wholeness through example rather than instruction. Their child inherits that alignment as the natural state of being.

Gabor Maté would call this the recovery of authenticity. He writes that healing occurs when we can be ourselves without fear of losing connection. Parenting becomes the daily practice of that authenticity. We stop teaching the child who to be and begin showing them what it means to be real. The home becomes a field where honesty and love grow together.

Alice Miller saw this as liberation from inherited shame. When we face our own truth without flinching, we free our children from carrying it. The pattern of hiding ends. The lineage of silence breaks. What was once passed down as pain becomes transmitted as presence. The next generation receives clarity instead of confusion.

Werner Erhard’s distinction between transformation and change completes this arc. Change rearranges behaviour; transformation alters being. The parent who has seen through the illusion no longer tries to change. They inhabit a different context, one of openness, trust, and simplicity. Every action arises from this state naturally. Life begins to feel guided rather than managed.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Stephen Porges’ language of co-regulation gives this transformation its biology. The calm adult becomes the body of safety that others can rest within. Their nervous system tells the truth without words. This is the ultimate repair, not a technique but a state of existence that radiates security. It is what love feels like when it no longer tries to prove itself.

The truth that remains is presence. It is the simplicity beneath every theory, the stillness beneath every method. When love and authenticity meet, the search for the good parent ends. What is left is a human being, alive, aware, and connected. A human being no longer striving to be perfect, simply willing to be real. That is the freedom at the heart of this book, the truth that was here from the beginning.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Where have you mistaken striving for love?
What does goodness mean to you now that perfection has lost its shape?
How does your body feel when you stop performing and begin to trust?
Which fears still whisper that love must be earned?
What would change if you believed that presence was already enough?
How might your child remember you, not as flawless, but as real?
What illusion are you ready to release so that truth can breathe?

Goodness was never the goal; it was the residue of truth.

The myth of the good parent dissolves in the light of presence. What remains is the real parent, alive and imperfect, guided by coherence instead of fear. Each breath, each repair, each act of return becomes a quiet proof that love does not need to perform itself to exist. As the search for goodness ends, a deeper rhythm begins — the rhythm of being human together, whole at last in the simplicity of connection.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
There is a moment, after all the searching, when the mind finally stops asking what to do. It pauses long enough to feel the ground beneath it. In that pause, parenting returns to what it was before the world began to measure it, a relationship between two beings learning how to stay connected through change. The work of becoming a good parent dissolves into the practice of being a real one.

John Bowlby called this reliability. Stephen Porges might call it regulation. Jean Liedloff would simply call it belonging. The language changes, yet the essence is the same. Safety, love, and coherence are not separate goals. They are different words for presence. The body knows this even when the mind forgets. When we slow down enough to listen, the knowing returns.

To parent from presence is to understand that guidance and growth are mutual. Every exchange teaches both sides how to love. The adult learns patience. The child learns trust. Both learn how to recover when connection falters. The rhythm of rupture and repair becomes the living heartbeat of family life, proof that relationship, not perfection, is what keeps us whole.

Daniel Siegel writes that integration is the final measure of wellbeing. Integration means difference held within unity. In the same way, a family becomes healthy not because everyone is the same but because everyone is seen. The home turns from a stage into a sanctuary. There is space for silence, laughter, conflict, and calm, all expressions of one ongoing conversation.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Gabor Maté reminds us that authenticity is the root of attachment. Love that cannot include truth becomes control. The parent who lives authentically teaches the child that honesty is safe. This, more than any method, ends the lineage of fear. It creates a culture where being yourself no longer risks rejection.

Alice Miller’s vision was similar. She believed that self-awareness in one generation frees the next from repeating pain. Awareness travels forward. What we make conscious in ourselves becomes unconscious safety in our children. This is how healing moves through time, quietly and invisibly, through presence.

Jean Liedloff’s continuum lives inside this truth. It is the return to rhythm after centuries of striving. When we belong to ourselves, we belong to one another. The child no longer carries the parent’s anxiety, and the parent no longer performs worth. Life flows again. Parenting stops being a task and becomes the natural expression of relationship restored.

Werner Erhard once said that transformation is not about changing life but letting life change you. That is the essence of this return. Presence transforms by revealing what has always been enough. The illusion of the good parent was never a failure. It was an invitation to remember that goodness and presence were never separate.

The work, if there is any, is to keep noticing. To pause when fear arises, to breathe before explanation, to choose awareness over reaction. Each time we do, the nervous system remembers safety and love remembers ease. Parenting then becomes what it was meant to be, not a performance, not a test, but a meeting of two people learning how to be human together.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text: And so the circle closes. The good parent was never missing. They were simply hidden beneath the noise of trying. In the stillness that follows, there is nothing left to become. There is only the presence that was waiting all along.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
The ideas in this book are not instructions but invitations. They are not meant to replace intuition but to help you recognise it. The compass that has guided this work can be used in everyday life, quietly and simply, as a way to return to coherence whenever uncertainty arises.

1. The Three I’s: Instinct, Intuition, Intellect

Each of these intelligences plays a vital part in calm guidance. When they work together, parenting becomes fluid and natural.

Instinct is the body’s first language. It feels before thought and alerts us to imbalance. Listening to instinct means trusting the body’s signals, not reacting from fear but pausing long enough to understand what they mean.

Intuition is the relational intelligence. It reads tone, atmosphere, and rhythm. It knows when to speak and when to stay quiet. Intuition thrives in stillness. The more we listen without rushing to fix, the clearer its guidance becomes.

Intellect is the mind’s organiser. It structures what instinct and intuition sense. It helps us communicate and reflect. When intellect serves the other two, it clarifies rather than controls. When it dominates, connection weakens. Balance comes from letting intellect describe what intuition already knows.

2. The Four Responses to Uncertainty

When connection feels threatened, the body moves automatically to protect itself. These responses are ancient and normal. Awareness turns them from reaction into information.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
Fight seeks to control the situation.
Flight avoids it.
Freeze withdraws until danger seems to pass.
Fawn appeases in order to restore peace.

In parenting, these responses often appear as correction, retreat, silence, or over-accommodation. The practice is to notice which pattern arises and to return to calm before acting. Regulation first, guidance second.

3. The Developing Brain

Understanding the child’s brain development dissolves much frustration. The prefrontal cortex, which allows reasoning and impulse control, matures slowly. A young child cannot think their way out of emotion. They need to borrow your nervous system until theirs can steady. Explanation rarely helps until calm has returned. Presence does. The parent’s body teaches what words cannot yet reach.

4. The Rhythm of Repair

Repair is the heartbeat of relationship. It is not about being right but about returning. Every reconnection after tension strengthens trust. Children remember the return more than the rupture. Calm after conflict becomes the architecture of safety. You do not have to fix; you only have to find your way back.

5. The Lineage of Awareness

Patterns travel through families, not as blame but as biology and habit. What we make conscious in ourselves stops being passed on unconsciously. Each act of awareness changes chemistry and atmosphere. When you stay calm where your parents could not, you alter inheritance. Healing does not erase history; it transforms it.

source: The Illusion of the Good Parent (book)
text:
6. The Compass in Daily Life

When tension rises, pause before reaction.
Notice your breath. Feel your feet. Allow space.
Ask which of the Three I’s is speaking.
Let the body settle before the mind decides.
Respond from coherence, not control.

Over time, this becomes effortless. The compass aligns itself through use. You begin to live by rhythm rather than rule, by presence rather than perfection. Parenting returns to its natural intelligence the intelligence of relationship.

7. For Those Who Work With Parents

If you support families, remember that insight alone rarely creates change. Regulation and relationship do. Meet parents where they are, without judgement. The nervous system learns safety through connection, not correction. Offer presence before advice. Transformation happens when people feel seen, not when they feel improved.

8. Further Reading

For deeper exploration of the ideas within this work:

- John Bowlby, Attachment and Loss

- Stephen Porges, The Polyvagal Theory

- Daniel J. Siegel, The Developing Mind

- Jean Liedloff, The Continuum Concept

- Gabor Maté, When the Body Says No

- Alice Miller, The Drama of the Gifted Child

- Werner Erhard, Transformation: The Context of Being

Each offers another language for the same truth — that presence heals more than perfection ever could.

9. Closing Thought

The compass does not point outward; it points home. Every pause, every breath, every act of returning is the practice of love remembering itself. When you trust what you already feel, life begins to guide you. The illusion fades. What remains is presence, steady and human, quietly enough.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: Introduction – In Search of Happiness: A Personal Reckoning (part 1)
text:
Parenting, for many of us, didn’t begin with a roadmap. It began with instinct, a deep, unshakable desire to love our children. Yet that desire often collides with confusion, exhaustion, and a gnawing sense that something isn’t quite working. This book was born from that collision, from a journey that has taken me across continents, careers, heartbreak, transformation, and ultimately into a deeper understanding of myself, my children, and what it truly means to be human.

I write this for you not as an expert standing above, but as a fellow seeker walking beside you. Especially if, like me, you have always strived for excellence, achieving in business, sport, creativity, or leadership, but still felt something missing. You know success, but crave depth. You value results, yet ache for something more meaningful in your parenting.

I first came across The Continuum Concept – In Search of Happiness Lost by Jean Liedloff while searching for answers to an ache I could not name. On the surface, I was successful, a British weightlifting champion, trained actor, business owner, coach, and more, but underneath there was a hollow space. Reading Liedloff’s work spoke to something ancient in me, making sense not to my intellect, but to my biology and nervous system.

Discovering that Jean was living in London, I met her for what she called “therapeutic conversations.” These sessions helped me understand the gap between the relationship I thought I had with my parents and the one I actually experienced. I learned why I had never felt safe enough to share my pain, and how I had lacked the secure base and co-regulation essential for healthy development.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: Introduction – In Search of Happiness: A Personal Reckoning (part 2)
text:
I vowed that if I ever became a father, I would apply the revolutionary insights I had gained from Jean. My aim would be to raise my children in a way that honoured biology, instinct, intuition, connection, and trust, and to share this with others.

Biologically aligned parenting, as Jean described, is not a technique, but a way of being. It honours the evolutionary expectations of the child’s brain, nervous system, and emotional body. Modern parenting, shaped heavily by behaviourist theories like those of B.F. Skinner, has drifted far from this model, often at the expense of a child’s deeper needs.

Psychologist Gordon Neufeld observes that when a child is difficult to parent, it signals a relationship that needs repair. Parenting, he insists, should be natural, intuitive, and joyful. Yet despite more parenting resources than ever before, many parents feel more overwhelmed than supported.

This book is not about blaming parents. It is about shining a light on what is often unseen, the blind spot that quietly shapes our children’s behaviour and our own confidence. It is about seeing clearly so we can choose differently.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: Parenting Is Not Supposed to Be This Hard
text:
We live in a time when parenting has never been more examined, more discussed, more advised upon. We have books, blogs, podcasts, and online communities dedicated to every stage and challenge. Yet despite this, many parents, especially those who are high achievers in other areas of life, end their days feeling drained, reactive, and uncertain.

The problem is not a lack of effort. The problem is that we are parenting in a way that no longer aligns with what our children’s biology expects. The mismatch between ancient design and modern practice makes even simple moments harder than they need to be.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: Jean Liedloff’s Discoveries Among the Yequana
text:
Jean Liedloff’s years with the Yequana were not a planned research project. They began with expeditions into the Amazon and evolved into a profound immersion in a culture that seemed to raise children without the struggles so common in the West.

Babies were held continuously for the first months, carried as parents worked, included in every aspect of life. They were not the constant centre of attention, but they were never left isolated. Their days were filled with the rhythms of adult life: movement, conversation, community.

The results were striking. Children did not cry without cause. They did not engage in constant defiance. They grew into cooperative, confident young people without the battles and bribes so common elsewhere.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
In our world, babies often sleep in separate rooms from the start. Parents return to work quickly. Time together is interrupted by devices and competing demands. We outsource parts of parenting to apps, toys, and programmes designed to entertain or “teach” our children, yet still we see rising rates of anxiety, behavioural struggles, and disconnection.

Dr Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory explains that the nervous system constantly scans for cues of safety or threat. For a child, the primary cue is the presence of a calm, attuned caregiver. If that presence is inconsistent, not in love but in how it is felt, the child’s system shifts towards vigilance. This can make them more reactive, more controlling, or more withdrawn.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
Much of modern parenting inherits its assumptions from B. F. Skinner’s work in behaviourism, the belief that behaviour can be shaped through rewards and punishments. While this can work to produce short-term compliance, it does not address the deeper emotional and relational needs that drive behaviour.

Attachment theorists like Bowlby, and contemporary voices like Gabor Maté and Gordon Neufeld, remind us that children’s willingness to follow our lead is rooted in relationship, not control. Yet without strong, consistent attunement, we fall back on behaviour management strategies that may control the surface but leave the underlying need unmet.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
High achievers bring a unique challenge to parenting. The skills that serve you in business, sport, or creative work, focus, efficiency, strategic thinking, are not the same skills that nurture a child’s emotional world.

Children do not respond predictably to systems of management. They measure safety in presence, not productivity. They thrive on repetition and rhythm, not novelty and disruption. When a high achiever applies their professional methods at home, they may inadvertently create pressure where there needs to be patience, or urgency where there needs to be understanding.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: The Biological Blueprint We Have Forgotten
text:
Human infants are born with expectations shaped over millions of years. They expect continuous presence from their caregivers, the steady rhythm of adult life around them, and the opportunity to borrow calm from the nervous systems of those who care for them. These are not luxuries; they are as essential to development as nutrition or shelter.

When these conditions are met, a child’s nervous system gradually develops the capacity to self-regulate. This is not a skill taught through explanation, but one built through repeated experience of safety in the presence of a regulated adult.

When these expectations are unmet, even when physical needs are provided, the child’s system treats the gap as a form of danger. They adapt in ways that can look like defiance, withdrawal, or anxiety. From the outside, it may appear to be just behaviour. From the inside, it is an adaptation to an environment that feels uncertain.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: How Modern Life Interrupts This Blueprint
text:
Modern life fragments the very conditions our children’s biology depends on. Work schedules pull parents away for most of the day. Screens compete for attention in the hours we are together. The extended family networks that once shared the load have thinned, leaving parents isolated.

The result is a form of scarcity, not of love but of attunement. As Dr Gordon Neufeld points out, when a child cannot consistently orient to the adults they are attached to, they will look elsewhere for guidance, often to peers or to themselves. This shift can happen far earlier than is healthy, leading to premature independence that looks mature but is actually a defensive adaptation.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: The Signs We Are Swimming Upstream
text:
Parents often sense when family life is harder than it should be. There may be constant negotiation over simple routines, emotional blow ups over small issues, or a general sense of tension in the air.

Dr Gabor Maté has said, “Children do not give us a hard time. They are having a hard time.” These struggles are not moral failings, nor are they signs of a broken child. They are signals that something in the environment is out of step with what the child’s biology expects.

Bedtime resistance, morning chaos, frequent power struggles, these are the surface ripples of a deeper current. Without recognising the current, we end up trying to manage the ripples one at a time, never realising they are part of the same pattern.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: Why This Matters Even More for High Achievers
text:
For high achievers, the belief that effort equals results runs deep. In most areas of life, that formula holds true. In parenting, it can backfire. When effort takes the form of increased control, more structure, or constant problem solving, it can unintentionally widen the gap between parent and child.

The child may experience this as pressure or mistrust, responding with more resistance or more withdrawal. The parent experiences it as confusion and frustration. The more they try, the less it seems to work, and the blind spot grows.

This is a dangerous loop for high achievers because it undermines the very confidence they are used to relying on. In business or sport, you can double down on effort and see improvement. In parenting, doubling down on the wrong framework deepens the problem.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
If parenting feels harder than it should, it is not a sign of failure. It is an invitation to look more closely. The struggles you see are not random. They are part of a coherent pattern, one that makes perfect sense once you understand the context in which it arises.

This book will guide you through that process. You will see where the blind spot comes from, how it hides in plain sight, and what it costs when it goes unnoticed.

We begin not with a list of tips, but with a shift in perspective. Once you see the blind spot, you cannot unsee it, and from that moment, everything changes.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: The First Signs You Might Be Missing Something
text:
It often begins in the most ordinary of moments. You are talking to your child about something simple, what’s for dinner, what time to leave for an event, when their response jars you. It might be sharper than expected, oddly flat, or laced with reluctance. You notice it, but you move on. Children have moods, after all.

But then it happens again. And again. Not every day, not in every interaction, but often enough to leave you with a faint sense that you are missing something.

I remember this with my youngest daughter when she was about ten. We were deciding whether to join friends for an afternoon activity. I thought I had left the choice to her, yet something in my tone must have signalled an expectation. Her “no” came with a defensiveness that felt disproportionate. I wondered, Why did that feel so much bigger than it was?

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: Why Obvious Behaviour Can Be Misleading
text:
Parenting culture trains us to focus on behaviour as the clearest indicator of what is happening for a child. Tantrums, refusals, outbursts, compliance, these are the signals we are told to read and respond to.

Jean Liedloff warned against this in The Continuum Concept – In Search of Happiness Lost. She observed that behaviour is “not the core of the problem, but the surface expression of something deeper.” When we take behaviour at face value, we risk misreading it entirely.

This is how the blind spot gains ground. It directs our attention to the visible, keeping the invisible out of view.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
Most parents rely on quick explanations to make sense of behaviour. They are tired. They are testing boundaries. They are just “that kind of child.” These narratives can be comforting because they give us a reason, even if it is not the right one.

Dr Gabor Maté notes, “We have become accustomed to seeing our children’s behaviour as separate from the emotional context in which it arises.” This is convenient, because addressing emotional context takes more time, attention, and reflection than managing the behaviour itself. But convenience can be costly.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: How Your Own Lens Shapes What You See
text:
None of us parent in a vacuum. Our view of our child’s behaviour is filtered through our own history, our own emotional triggers, and the beliefs we carry from how we were raised. These filters can make certain behaviours stand out sharply while rendering others invisible.

I grew up in a home where certain expressions of emotion were acceptable and others were quietly discouraged. Without realising it, I carried those same unwritten rules into my parenting. My daughters could be expressive in some ways, but there were tones and responses that I reacted to more strongly, not because they were wrong, but because they pressed against old boundaries I did not know were still there.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
It might seem harmless to misinterpret a shrug here or a sigh there. But over time, these small misreads accumulate. Each one slightly alters the way you respond to your child, and each altered response slightly shifts the way they respond to you.

Dr Gordon Neufeld often says that “Children are meaning-makers.” They interpret not just our words, but our tone, body language, and emotional availability. When our read of their behaviour is off, we send signals we do not intend. They make meaning from those signals, and that meaning influences their future behaviour.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
Every family has an emotional climate. Most of the time, you do not notice it, just as you do not notice the air until it changes. But children are attuned to these shifts with astonishing sensitivity.

It might be as simple as the way you set down a plate on the table, the pause before answering a question, or the slight edge in your voice when you are distracted. These small signals tell your child something about your internal state, and they respond to that state whether you are aware of it or not.

When these signals are consistently tinged with stress, irritation, or impatience, the atmosphere changes. The child adjusts to this atmosphere, not consciously, but through their behaviour and emotional tone.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: When You Start to Feel Uncertain
text:
As these small shifts build up, you may notice a change in yourself. Where you once felt confident in handling situations, you now feel tentative. You second-guess your words. You wonder whether you are being too strict, too soft, too distracted.

This uncertainty is not a flaw in your character. It is a signal. When your responses no longer produce the outcomes you expect, your confidence begins to erode. The blind spot thrives here, because the more uncertain you feel, the more reactive you are likely to become, and the more reactive you are, the stronger the dynamic becomes.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
The unseen parenting dynamic works in a loop. The child’s behaviour triggers something in the parent. The parent’s reaction reinforces something in the child. Over time, these reactions become patterns.

Behaviourists like B. F. Skinner showed how repeated cycles of stimulus and response can become self-sustaining. In the home, this means a child may react to your tone more than your actual words, and you may react to their reaction more than to the situation itself. Neither of you may realise that the original trigger was not the behaviour at all, but the state you were both in before the moment began.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: How It Feels from the Child’s Side
text:
From the child’s perspective, the pattern may feel like a set of unspoken rules. Certain topics are risky. Certain tones mean trouble. Certain moments are better avoided altogether. They learn to read you quickly, adjusting their behaviour not only to avoid conflict, but to manage your reactions.

This is rarely conscious, but it has consequences. The more energy they put into managing you, the less is available for the developmental work of learning who they are and how to manage themselves.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
If the blind spot remains unnoticed, the dynamic becomes part of the fabric of your relationship. The small moments of misunderstanding, the subtle shifts in tone, the guardedness in conversation, they start to feel normal.

Over time, the child’s behaviour may harden into patterns that look like defiance, withdrawal, or indifference. Your own responses may become less curious, more controlling, or more resigned. Without realising it, you both adjust to a version of your relationship that is less open, less trusting, and less connected than it could be.

And because it has happened gradually, you might not even remember when it began.

Chapter 3 - The Parenting Blind Spot in Action

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: The Ordinary Moments That Don’t Feel Ordinary
text:
The blind spot doesn’t reveal itself through dramatic breakdowns or sudden crises. More often, it shows up in the small exchanges that leave you unsettled. A child refusing to come to dinner when called. A teenager rolling their eyes at a simple request. A conversation that ends in slammed doors rather than understanding.

I recall evenings when my eldest daughter, then fifteen, would sit reading long after midnight. I would remind her that it was late, my words intended as care. But the tension in my voice betrayed my anxiety. Her sharp retort stung. On the surface it was about bedtime. Underneath, it was about regulation, trust, and signals neither of us fully understood.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
High achieving parents are used to seeing effort produce results. Train harder and your performance improves. Work longer hours and the project moves forward. But in parenting, effort can feel like pouring water into sand. The more you try, the less you see in return.

Gordon Neufeld and Gabor Maté describe this mismatch in Hold On to Your Kids. Children do not respond in linear ways. They respond to relationship, not technique. If the connection is strained, even the most carefully worded guidance may bounce off unheard.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
Many of us lean on what we know. Discipline systems. Rewards. Consequences. Persuasion. These tools, borrowed from behaviourism and popular culture, may produce short term compliance. But the long term cost is significant.

B. F. Skinner showed how behaviour could be conditioned by reinforcement. Yet applying these methods at home reduces the child to a subject of control rather than a participant in relationship. Jean Liedloff warned that this approach contradicts the child’s natural expectation of inclusion and trust.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: The Blind Spot as a Loop
text:
Once triggered, the blind spot tends to reinforce itself. A child resists. The parent insists. The child withdraws. The parent escalates. Round and round it goes.

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory helps explain why. When a parent’s nervous system shifts into stress, their tone, posture, and micro expressions signal danger to the child. The child’s system responds in kind, entering fight, flight, or freeze. Each nervous system is reacting to the other, amplifying the cycle.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
This is where self doubt creeps in. You wonder whether you are too harsh, too lenient, too inconsistent. Your inner critic grows louder with each failed attempt to steer things back on course.

For high achievers, this doubt cuts deep. Success elsewhere is built on confidence and decisiveness. At home, uncertainty can feel like weakness. The temptation is to double down, to control harder. But this only deepens the blind spot.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
Children are not passive in this loop. They are interpreting our signals constantly. A sigh becomes a sign of disappointment. A pause feels like rejection. They create meaning from fragments, and those meanings shape their behaviour.

John Bowlby’s work on attachment showed how children orient to caregivers for safety. When that orientation feels inconsistent, the child adapts defensively. What looks like defiance may be protection. What looks like indifference may be shielding.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
The greatest cost of the blind spot is not the arguments or the defiance. It is the gradual erosion of trust. Trust is not lost in one moment but in the accumulation of many small ones.

Jean Liedloff noted that Yequana children did not question their parents’ leadership. It was woven into the fabric of daily life through consistency, presence, and attunement. In our culture, where signals are mixed and stress is high, trust can thin without anyone realising until the gap feels wide.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: When You Feel Out of Options
text:
Parents describe this as hitting a wall. Nothing seems to work. Strategies contradict each other. Advice feels confusing. The sense of failure grows.

Dr Gabor Maté emphasises that many so called behavioural problems are actually attachment problems. When the relationship is not secure, the child’s energy goes into resistance rather than growth. Seeing this changes everything, but until then, the wall feels real.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
If unaddressed, the blind spot can become the default. Conversations grow shorter. Conflicts repeat. Laughter fades. Both parent and child retreat into roles neither wanted but both accept as normal.

Neufeld warns that when children turn prematurely to peers for orientation, parents lose their natural place of influence. The drift may begin in adolescence, but its roots lie earlier in patterns unnoticed.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: Recognising Yourself in the Blind Spot
text:
The hardest part is admitting that the blind spot exists not only in your child’s behaviour but in your own. It is not about blame. It is about awareness. Once seen, it cannot be unseen.

The blind spot is not a personal failing. It is a predictable outcome of parenting in a culture disconnected from our biological blueprint. Recognising it is the first step to change.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: The Moment You Realise Something Isn’t Adding Up
text:
It often happens in the quietest moments. The house is still, the laptop closed, the bank account reassuring. You have created the life you thought you were working for: freedom, space, and the ability to be present for your children in a way your own parents might have dreamed of.

And yet, there it is. That knot in your stomach when your child pushes back. The way your voice tightens when they roll their eyes, ignore you, or retreat into their own world. The moment when you think, Why is this so hard?

I remember sitting at the kitchen table one rainy afternoon, watching one of my daughters refuse to join in with something I thought would be fun. I had given her the option, kept my tone light, even offered to do it together. Still, she resisted, not defiantly, but with that quiet, stubborn energy children can summon when they are set on their own course.

Back then, I did not have the language for it, but I could feel the gap between us. I had the time, the resources, the willingness, and still, something in our connection faltered.

This is where many high-achieving parents find themselves. You have cleared the biggest hurdles in business or career, but the day-to-day reality of parenting brings you up against a wall you did not see coming. It is not about money. It is not about time. It is about something deeper, and the fact that you cannot quite put your finger on it is the part that keeps you awake at night.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: The Moment You Realise Something Isn’t Adding Up
text: This is the blind spot in the mirror, the thing you cannot see even when you are looking straight at it.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: Why Success in One Arena Does Not Guarantee Success in Another
text:
It is tempting to believe that the traits that made you thrive in business: discipline, vision, persistence, the ability to solve problems, will naturally translate into parenting. After all, in both arenas you are guiding something toward a goal.

But children are not projects. They do not respond to quarterly targets, best practice protocols, or even the kind of incentive systems that work in teams.

I learned this the hard way. In my early years as a parent, I tried to apply the same strategise and execute approach I had used in work. I thought that if I explained enough, modelled enough, offered the right mix of freedom and structure, my children would respond exactly as planned.

What I did not expect was the way my children sometimes pushed back, ignored what I thought was a perfectly reasonable request, or seemed utterly unmoved by the carefully designed motivators I had put in place.

That is when I started digging into the history of modern parenting and discovered something unsettling. Much of what passes for good parenting today is rooted in mid-20th-century behaviourism, shaped by thinkers like B. F. Skinner, whose methods were designed for lab animals, not humans in families. Reward and punishment, praise and consequence, they were never designed to nurture the complex emotional worlds of children.

High-achieving parents often fall into this trap without knowing it. We are used to systems that deliver results quickly. But when those systems fail with our children, we double down and the gap between us widens.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: Why Success in One Arena Does Not Guarantee Success in Another
text: It is not that our skills do not matter. It is that they are built for a different kind of arena. And parenting is a game where the unspoken rules are older, deeper, and far more personal than any business manual can teach.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: How the Blind Spot Hides in Everyday Moments
text:
The parenting blind spot does not usually announce itself with a siren or flashing light. It is quieter than that, woven into the fabric of daily life.

It shows up in the sigh you let out when your child interrupts your work for the fourth time in an hour. In the way you tighten your voice just a little when they forget to do the thing you asked. In the small calculations you make: If I let this slide now, will they ever learn?

In my own home, I began to notice it during the most ordinary routines: meal times, bedtimes, the morning rush. Moments when I thought I was teaching, guiding, or instilling discipline, but where I was actually reacting to my own discomfort at things not going as planned.

That is the nature of this blind spot. It does not live in the extremes, the full-blown meltdown or the outright defiance, but in the micro-moments we barely register. The look you give. The way you withdraw. The words you choose when you think you are just being clear.

Because these moments are so small, they slip past our awareness. We do not connect them to the bigger patterns of tension, disconnection, or constant negotiation in our homes. And yet, to our children, they matter immensely.

For high-achieving parents, this is especially tricky. We are trained to notice the big wins and the glaring losses. Subtlety does not always get our attention. But in parenting, the subtleties are the terrain.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: How the Blind Spot Hides in Everyday Moments
text: The blind spot thrives here, in the space between what you believe you are communicating and what your child is actually receiving. It is not malicious. It is not neglectful. It is simply unseen. And until you see it, you cannot steer differently.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: The Hidden Cost of Not Seeing It
text:
When you miss the blind spot, life does not usually implode overnight. That is what makes it so deceptive. The cost builds slowly, like a slow leak you do not notice until the floorboards start to buckle.

The first cost is emotional drift. You may still spend plenty of time together, but the ease you once felt begins to thin out. Your child becomes more cautious in what they share. You notice them testing your mood before they speak.

The second is increased resistance. Not all at once, but in small escalations: more negotiation over simple requests, more sighs, eye rolls, or subtle pushback. You tell yourself this is just the age they are at, but deep down you wonder if you are losing influence.

And then there is self-doubt. As the patterns repeat, you start to question your instincts. You lean more heavily on parenting advice, rules, and strategies that may not even feel natural to you. The more you try to get it right, the more disconnected you can feel.

If left unchecked, the blind spot becomes a quiet divider. It does not destroy the relationship, but it keeps you from building the kind of bond that withstands the storms of adolescence and the pull of independence.

For high-achievers, there is an added twist: you are used to spotting problems early in business and fixing them fast. But at home, this one escapes your radar. And by the time you notice its effects, the habits are already well-worn.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: The Hidden Cost of Not Seeing It
text: This is not about blame. It is about the reality that the costs are not just in the present, they compound into the future. And the sooner you can see the blind spot, the sooner you can choose differently.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: Why High-Achievers Are Especially Prone to This
text:
High-achievers often thrive because they can see around corners in their work. You anticipate obstacles, plan for contingencies, and optimise every process. But parenting does not follow the same rules, and that is part of the trap.

Your professional instincts can actually mask the blind spot. In business, when something is not working, you gather data, apply a strategy, and measure the results. At home, this can turn into treating your child like a project instead of a person who is still finding their footing.

It is not malicious, it is muscle memory. You have built a life by being proactive and decisive. But children do not respond to efficiency the way teams or markets do. The subtle signals they give off, a shift in tone, a pause before answering, a hint of withdrawal, can easily get overlooked when you are focused on moving things forward.

Another reason high-achievers are prone to this is time consciousness. Even if you are time-rich now, years of fitting everything into tight schedules can leave you with a low tolerance for drawn-out conversations, messy emotions, or resistance. You want resolution now, so you reach for tools that give quick compliance, but those tools often plant the seeds of the very behaviours you want to avoid.

Finally, high-achievers are vulnerable to confirmation bias. You have succeeded by trusting your judgement, so it is harder to accept that something you have always believed, perhaps about discipline, motivation, or respect, might not be serving your child in the way you imagine.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: Why High-Achievers Are Especially Prone to This
text: The irony is that the same drive and focus that built your success could easily be the strengths that help you address the blind spot, once you are able to see it clearly.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: The Myths That Keep the Blind Spot Hidden
text:
Blind spots rarely exist in a vacuum. They are propped up by cultural myths, family narratives, and common sense advice that has been passed down for generations, much of it unquestioned. These myths are powerful because they feel true, especially when they have been reinforced by your own upbringing or by voices you respect.

One of the most persistent myths is “Children need to be taught who is in charge.” This idea assumes that respect comes from hierarchy rather than relationship. It drives parents toward strategies that assert authority quickly: raising your voice, laying down strict rules, removing privileges. In the short term, you may see compliance. In the long term, these tactics can weaken the trust you actually want to build.

Another myth is “Good behaviour should be rewarded.” It sounds harmless, even motivating, but it trains children to perform for approval rather than develop their own internal compass. High-achievers, who have often thrived on external validation, may find this particularly hard to question. Yet it creates a dependency on praise that can undermine resilience when life inevitably gets harder.

Then there is “If they do not learn now, they never will.” This fuels urgency and impatience, encouraging corrective action in the moment rather than allowing for gradual learning through lived experience. It assumes that every misstep needs a fix, rather than being part of a developmental process.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: The Myths That Keep the Blind Spot Hidden
text:
A subtler myth is “I turned out fine, so these methods work.” This one is tricky because it appeals to lived experience and to pride. But fine is a low bar. The truth is, most adults carry unexamined habits, triggers, and coping strategies shaped by their own upbringing. Without reflection, these are passed on, blind spot intact.

Finally, the most seductive myth for high-achievers: “If I just work harder at this, I will get it right.” This turns parenting into a performance metric. It is the instinct to optimise, troubleshoot, and double down, without pausing to consider that the problem might not be in your effort, but in the framework you are using.

These myths are comfortable because they fit neatly into the achievement mindset. But they are also the very thing that keeps the blind spot from being noticed, until the behaviours you want to avoid become impossible to ignore.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: When the Blind Spot Starts to Show
text:
Blind spots are invisible until they are not. At first, the signs might seem small: an eye roll, a sarcastic comment, a sudden reluctance to share what is going on in their day. You might chalk it up to just a phase or tiredness. But over time, these moments multiply.

One of the first giveaways is increased resistance. Requests that were once met with a quick okay are now met with hesitation, negotiation, or outright refusal. It can feel baffling, especially if you have worked hard to be present and supportive.

Another sign is emotional withdrawal. Your child may spend more time in their room, avoid eye contact, or give clipped answers. It is easy to interpret this as moodiness, and sometimes it is, but it can also be a signal that they do not feel understood or safe to be fully themselves.

Then there is performative compliance. On the surface, they are doing what is asked, but there is a flatness to it. No joy, no curiosity, just a sense of ticking boxes. This can be particularly deceptive because it looks like things are going smoothly, when in reality, the relationship is quietly fraying.

For high-achieving parents, the blind spot often shows up in repeating conversations. You find yourself giving the same reminders, explanations, or pep talks, and still nothing changes. This breeds frustration on both sides, as your child feels micro-managed and you feel unheard.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: When the Blind Spot Starts to Show
text:
Finally, the most confronting sign: they stop coming to you. The little disclosures: what happened at school, a problem with a friend, a funny story, begin to dry up. They turn to peers, online spaces, or keep things to themselves entirely. By the time you notice, the distance can feel like a chasm.

These moments do not appear overnight. They accumulate quietly until one day, you realise you are on the outside of your child’s inner world, unsure how you got there. That is the power and the danger of a parenting blind spot.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: Why Success Can Make the Blind Spot Worse
text:
It is one of the great ironies of parenting as a high achiever: the very qualities that made you successful in business can work against you at home.

In your professional world, drive, precision, and systems create results. If a plan is not working, you adjust, optimise, or double down. You expect consistent output because you have built structures designed to produce it. With children, though, these instincts can backfire.

Success can amplify the blind spot in three ways:

Overconfidence in Transferable Skills: High achievers often assume that because they can lead teams, close deals, or solve complex problems, they can apply the same methods to parenting. It is not arrogance, it is logic. But children do not respond to management strategies in the same way employees or projects do. They are not motivated by KPIs or quarterly goals.

Invisible Pressure: Success can create an unspoken standard of excellence that seeps into family life. You may never say you must achieve, but the atmosphere of high performance is there. For some children, this becomes motivating. For others, it becomes suffocating. And when they push back, it can feel like defiance rather than a natural need for autonomy.

Time and Energy Miscalculation: Once you have made it, you imagine that more time with your children will automatically translate into better relationships. But here is the hidden snag: the habits, pace, and intensity of your professional life do not dissolve just because you are at home. Your mind can still be elsewhere: running scenarios, solving problems, planning ahead, which your child reads as absence, even when you are in the same room.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: Why Success Can Make the Blind Spot Worse
text:
The trap here is subtle. The more you double down on what has worked for you in the past, the more entrenched the blind spot becomes. You are operating at full competence in your own mind, yet missing the signals your child is sending in theirs.

And when results do not appear, when the behaviour does not improve, the respect does not grow, the closeness does not return, the temptation is to try harder. But in parenting, harder is not always better. Sometimes, it is the very thing widening the gap.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: The Illusion of “I Am Doing Enough”
text:
It is easy to believe that your effort equals effectiveness. In business, that equation works more often than not: you put in the hours, you see the results.

Parenting breaks that formula.

Many high-achieving parents fall into the belief that because they:

- Spend more time at home than most

- Provide well materially

- Organise family life efficiently

- Offer advice, guidance, and encouragement regularly

…they must be doing enough.

But here is the hidden truth: enough in parenting is not measured by quantity alone. Children notice something deeper than the number of hours, the holidays, or the quality of the school you provide. They sense whether you are truly with them, in presence, not just proximity.

The illusion of doing enough is reinforced when no major crises are happening. Your child is clothed, fed, doing homework, and not causing serious disruption. It is tempting to take this as confirmation that all is well.

But just as in business, where a company can look profitable while quietly eroding its market share, a family can appear stable while emotional connection and trust quietly thin out.

This illusion is dangerous because it:

- Masks Early Warning Signs: You miss subtle signals, the shorter answers, the reluctance to share feelings, the change in body language, because they do not feel urgent.

- Breeds Complacency: The absence of obvious problems convinces you there is no need to adjust.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: The Illusion of “I Am Doing Enough”
text:
- Delays Intervention: By the time clear behavioural issues show up, disrespect, withdrawal, defiance, they are more entrenched and harder to shift.
