The enough you think you are doing might be plenty for a colleague, an employee, or even a friend. But for your child, the equation is entirely different. And without realising it, you might be operating at a deficit your child feels every day, even if they cannot articulate it.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: When the Blind Spot Becomes the New Normal
text:
Blind spots are not always sudden realisations. Most often, they form quietly and then solidify until they feel like the way things have always been.

In parenting, this is particularly risky because children adapt quickly. They adjust to the emotional climate you set, even if that climate is missing vital elements for their growth.

What starts as:

- Skipping deeper conversations because you are tired

- Relying on instructions instead of shared moments

- Postponing play or connection for when things calm down

…can, over time, become the rhythm of family life.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
In business, you might call this mission creep, small, almost invisible shifts that take you away from your original goal. The danger is that these shifts rarely feel like crises, so you do not rally to correct them.

In families, the drift often looks like:

- More functional talk, less laughter

- More managing your child, less discovering who they are

- More reacting to behaviour, less exploring what drives it

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: The Cost of Normalising the Drift
text:
Children may accept the distance, but acceptance is not the same as thriving. What they internalise can follow them into adulthood: relationships that lack trust, difficulty expressing needs, or a constant background hum of self-doubt.

And because these patterns often emerge without open conflict, they are harder to spot and easier to justify.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)

If your company was running with outdated systems, you would notice the creeping inefficiency only when a competitor

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: Why We Parent the Way We Were Parent
text:
No matter how many books we read or podcasts we listen to, most of us parent in ways that echo our own upbringing. Some of it is deliberate, some of it unconscious. The tone we use, the way we respond to defiance, even the very definition of what it means to be a “good parent” is shaped by the blueprint we absorbed as children.

I remember realising this when my daughters were very young. I had promised myself I would not repeat certain patterns, yet there they were, surfacing in the heat of the moment. A sharpness in my voice, a command that sounded too much like my father’s, a withdrawal that echoed my mother’s silences. The blind spot is not just about what we do, but about the scripts written long before we became parents.

Attachment theory, pioneered by John Bowlby, explains much of this. Children form internal working models based on their caregivers. These models shape how we expect relationships to feel. If warmth and co-regulation were missing in our childhood, we may unknowingly struggle to provide them to our own children.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
Beyond our personal upbringing lies the cultural story. Behaviourism, spearheaded by B. F. Skinner, left a deep imprint on parenting norms. Rewards, punishments, star charts, time-outs became standard practice, not because they worked long-term, but because they fit the industrial logic of efficiency and control.

Jean Liedloff, in The Continuum Concept – In Search of Happiness Lost, revealed how far this cultural drift has taken us from our evolutionary design. Living among the Yequana, she saw children raised without coercion or conditioning. They were carried, included, trusted. Their behaviour flowed naturally from the secure web of relationship and community around them. No star charts, no time-outs, and no blind spot in sight.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory sheds light on why our childhood patterns linger. Our nervous systems remember. If we grew up in households where calm regulation was rare, our bodies carry that imprint. We may intend to be patient, but under stress our system defaults to old patterns.

Co-regulation is the missing piece. Children rely on borrowing their parent’s calm until their own system matures. When parents are dysregulated, even with the best of intentions, the child cannot find safety in their signals. The blind spot is not only cultural, it is physiological.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
section: Why High Achievers Carry It Forward
text:
For high achievers, the blind spot is often magnified. Many of us channelled our childhood wounds into performance. We became excellent at achievement, but less fluent in emotional safety. Success became our way of managing insecurity.

That drive produces extraordinary results in business, sport, and art. But at home, it creates pressure. Children do not thrive under constant metrics of success. They need presence, not performance. And yet, the high achiever’s instinct is to fix, to measure, to optimise. In doing so, we may repeat the very patterns we swore we would leave behind.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
It is common to believe we can simply decide to parent differently. But willpower is rarely enough. Neuroscience tells us that stress returns us to default wiring. Unless we actively build new patterns of regulation, the old ones resurface.

This is why many parents find themselves sounding like their own parents despite their intentions. It is not weakness. It is the nervous system replaying what it knows. The blind spot is stubborn because it is embodied.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
What makes this so challenging is that much of it happens beneath awareness. We do not announce to our children, “I am carrying my father’s impatience” or “I am repeating my mother’s withdrawal.” It happens in tone, in timing, in micro-expressions. Our children absorb it without words.

Daniel Siegel calls this implicit memory, patterns that live in us without conscious recall. These implicit memories create our blind spots, repeating what we have not brought into awareness.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
Left unexamined, the blind spot ripples outward. We recreate the very dynamics we longed to escape. Our children feel the same confusion we once felt. Why does love sometimes feel sharp? Why does comfort sometimes feel absent?

This is not about blaming our parents. They, too, were shaped by their culture and upbringing. It is about recognising the chain and daring to interrupt it.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
To cope, we often create stories. “I turned out fine.” “They did their best.” “It made me stronger.” These stories help us move forward, but they can also mask the pain that fuels the blind spot. Fine is not the same as whole. Strong is not the same as secure.

Jean Liedloff’s work with the Yequana exposed the fallacy of these stories. She showed that children raised in alignment with their continuum expectations do not need to toughen up to survive disconnection. They thrive because connection is never in doubt.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text: Seeing the roots of the blind spot is not comfortable. It means acknowledging that the patterns we dislike in ourselves did not begin with us. They are inherited from parents, culture, history. And yet, discomfort is the doorway. Awareness begins when we admit that what we carry may not be ours, but we are responsible for what we pass on.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
The blind spot is not just about today’s conflicts. It is about the legacy we leave. If we ignore it, we risk transmitting confusion, insecurity, and dysregulation to the next generation. If we face it, we create the possibility of something different.

Children do not need perfect parents. They need parents willing to see. Parents who can look honestly at their own inheritance and choose differently. This is where change begins, not with technique, but with courage.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
When Doing Everything Right Still Feels Wrong Parenting blind spots often hide in the space between our effort and our exhaustion. You are doing all the right things — providing, showing up, making time, giving advice — yet inside you feel drained. The paradox is striking: the more you do, the less you feel it is working.

Gabor Maté writes, “We get so busy doing that we lose the being in our lives — and yet, children only learn from who we are, not what we do.” This is the essence of depletion. When we confuse doing for being, we miss the deeper signal our children are sending.

The Illusion of Coping Depletion rarely looks like collapse. It looks like holding it together. From the outside, all is fine: the bills are paid, the meals are cooked, the routines are kept. Inside, however, something vital is missing. The blind spot hides here, in the illusion that coping equals thriving.

Children know the difference. They feel when a parent’s smile is tired, when laughter is forced, when presence is hollow. Gordon Neufeld reminds us that children are fuelled by presence, not by provision. Depletion erodes presence first, even when practical care continues.

How Depletion Disguises Itself For high achievers, depletion can be especially well hidden. Years of training in resilience and persistence make exhaustion feel normal. You push through, meet deadlines, deliver results. But children do not interpret this as strength. They interpret it as absence.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
Jean Liedloff observed this contrast. Among the Yequana, parents did not measure success by doing more. They trusted the flow of life, confident in their innate capacity to live harmoniously with their children. In the modern world, we measure, manage, and schedule until harmony is lost.

The Nervous System Toll Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory helps explain why depletion is so costly. The nervous system cannot fake regulation. A parent who is chronically stressed may hide it with words, but their body betrays them — in tone, in posture, in micro-expressions. Children tune into these cues more than the content of our speech.

Gabor Maté has noted that “when we are cut off from our own feelings, we lose our compass.” The cost of depletion is not only emotional; it is physiological. Stress embeds itself in the body, showing up as tension, irritability, disrupted sleep. Parenting blind spots are lived in the body as much as in the mind.

Cultural Myths That Fuel Depletion Our culture reinforces depletion with myths that sound noble but are corrosive:

- Good parents always put children first.

- Love is enough.

- Workplace resilience transfers home.

- Asking for help is weakness.

- Children’s success equals parental success.

These myths keep parents striving, even as their inner reserves run dry. They blur the line between dedication and self-abandonment.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
When Control Replaces Connection As presence erodes, many parents compensate with control. Gordon Neufeld describes this shift: true authority flows from connection, but when depleted, parents often grasp at power. Control can create short-term compliance, but at the cost of long-term trust.

The more depleted you feel, the more tempting it is to reach for rules, consequences, and quick fixes. The blind spot deepens here: children are responding to your depletion, not to the logic of your rules.

The Unseen Impact on Children Children adapt to parental depletion, but adaptation is not the same as thriving. They may become overly compliant, hiding their needs to avoid burdening you. Or they may become more demanding, escalating behaviour to get your attention. Either way, they are adjusting to absence rather than being nourished by presence.

Jean Liedloff wrote, “We have lost confidence in our innate capacity to live harmoniously with our children.” When depletion takes hold, harmony is replaced by survival — both for the parent and the child.

The Body as Messenger The blind spot of depletion is not simply psychological. It is somatic. Gabor Maté has observed that ignoring our needs is not selflessness but self-abandonment. The body keeps the score: tension in the shoulders, shallow breath, fatigue that no amount of sleep erases.

These signals are not failures. They are messengers. They are reminders that parenting is not sustainable without presence, and presence cannot exist without replenishment.

source: The Parenting Blind Spot (book)
text:
Stories of Change I recall a father I once spoke with who told me, “I realised I was measuring myself by tasks completed. When I read Liedloff, I saw that my son needed me to measure myself by moments connected.” His story captures the shift from depletion to awareness. The blind spot was not his lack of effort, but his misdirected measure of success.

The Compass of Awareness Awareness is the turning point. Neufeld describes parenting as a compass rather than a set of techniques. When the compass is distorted by depletion, we drift. When we replenish, we regain orientation.

The cost of depletion is high, but it is not irreversible. The first step is to see it, to name it, to admit that doing everything is not the same as being present. Awareness itself begins to restore the compass.

source: The Parenting Assumption (book)
